paragraph: The long-term benefit of the approach taken for project MinE is the priceless catalogue of many non-ALS whole genomes that can be used to investigate other human diseases, including Diabetes Mellitus,<ref>{{cite journal | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0026953 | volume=6 | issue=11 | title=Association of Six Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms with Gestational Diabetes Mellitus in a Chinese Population | journal=PLOS ONE | article-number=e26953 | pmid=22096510 | pmc=3214026 | last1 = Wang | first1 = Y | last2 = Nie | first2 = M | last3 = Li | first3 = W | last4 = Ping | first4 = F | last5 = Hu | first5 = Y | last6 = Ma | first6 = L | last7 = Gao | first7 = J | last8 = Liu | first8 = J| year=2011 | bibcode=2011PLoSO...626953W | doi-access=free }}</ref> some types of cancer, and other [[neurological disorder]]s.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Seshadri | first1 = Sudha | display-authors = etal   | year = 2010 | title = Genome-wide Analysis of Genetic Loci Associated With Alzheimer Disease | journal = JAMA | volume = 303 | issue = 18| pages = 1832–1840 | doi = 10.1001/jama.2010.574| pmc = 2989531 | pmid=20460622}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | pmc = 1271381 | pmid=16252231 | doi=10.1086/496902 | volume=77 | issue=5 | title=High-resolution whole-genome association study of Parkinson disease | year=2005 | journal=Am. J. Hum. Genet. | pages=685–93 | last1 = Maraganore | first1 = DM | last2 = de Andrade | first2 = M |author2-link=Mariza de Andrade| last3 = Lesnick | first3 = TG | last4 = Strain | first4 = KJ | last5 = Farrer | first5 = MJ | last6 = Rocca | first6 = WA | last7 = Pant | first7 = PV | last8 = Frazer | first8 = KA | last9 = Cox | first9 = DR | last10 = Ballinger | first10 = DG}}</ref> Project MinE is worldwide the largest genetic study for Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis.  The work has started in the second quarter of 2013 and is a unique international collaboration between scientists, industry, social foundations and patients. On July 25, 2016, the first results were published in 2 publications in [[Nature Genetics]] leading to the discovery of [[NEK1]] and C21orf2 as new ALS risk genes.<ref>{{Cite journal|doi=10.1038/ng.3622|title=Genome-wide association analyses identify new risk variants and the genetic architecture of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis|year=2016|last1=Van Rheenen|first1=Wouter|last2=Shatunov|first2=Aleksey|last3=Dekker|first3=Annelot M.|last4=McLaughlin|first4=Russell L.|last5=Diekstra|first5=Frank P.|last6=Pulit|first6=Sara L.|last7=Van Der Spek|first7=Rick A A.|last8=Võsa|first8=Urmo|last9=De Jong|first9=Simone|last10=Robinson|first10=Matthew R.|last11=Yang|first11=Jian|last12=Fogh|first12=Isabella|last13=Van Doormaal|first13=Perry TC|last14=Tazelaar|first14=Gijs H P.|last15=Koppers|first15=Max|last16=Blokhuis|first16=Anna M.|last17=Sproviero|first17=William|last18=Jones|first18=Ashley R.|last19=Kenna|first19=Kevin P.|last20=Van Eijk|first20=Kristel R.|last21=Harschnitz|first21=Oliver|last22=Schellevis|first22=Raymond D.|last23=Brands|first23=William J.|last24=Medic|first24=Jelena|last25=Menelaou|fir

ref_block: <ref name="Jama">{{cite journal | vauthors = Maciejewski PK, Zhang B, Block SD, Prigerson HG | title = An empirical examination of the stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 7 | pages = 716–723 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17312291 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.7.716 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
paragraph: A widely cited 2003 study of bereaved individuals conducted by Maciejewski and colleagues at [[Yale University]] obtained some findings consistent with a five-stage hypothesis but others inconsistent with it. Several letters were also published in the same journal criticizing this research and arguing against the stage idea.<ref name="Jama">{{cite journal | vauthors = Maciejewski PK, Zhang B, Block SD, Prigerson HG | title = An empirical examination of the stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 7 | pages = 716–723 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17312291 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.7.716 | doi-access = free }}</ref> It was pointed out, for example, that instead of "acceptance" being the final stage of grieving, the data actually showed it was the most frequently endorsed item at the first and every other time point measured;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bonanno GA, Boerner K | title = The stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 24 | pages = 2693; author reply 2693–4 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17595267 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.24.2693-a }}</ref> that cultural and geographical bias within the sample population was not controlled for;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Weiner JS | title = The stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 24 | pages = 2692–93; author reply 2693–4 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17595265 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.24.2692-b }}</ref> and that out of the total number of participants originally recruited for the study, nearly 40% were excluded from the analysis who did not fit the stage model.<ref name="Silver"/> In subsequent work, Prigerson & Maciejewski focused on acceptance (emotional and cognitive) and backed away from stages, writing that their earlier results "might more accurately be described as 'states' of grief."<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Prigerson HG, Maciejewski PK | title = Grief and acceptance as opposite sides of the same coin: setting a research agenda to study peaceful acceptance of loss | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 193 | issue = 6 | pages = 435–437 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 19043142 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.bp.108.053157 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1001/jama.297.7.716 |title=An Empirical Examination of the Stage Theory of Grief |year=2007 |last1=MacIejewski |first1=P. K. |last2=Zhang |first2=B. |last3=Block |first3=S. D. |last4=Prigerson |first4=H. G. |journal=JAMA: The Journal of the American Medical Association |volume=297 |issue=7 |pages=716–23 |pmid=17312291|doi-access=free }}</ref>
paragraph: This [[model]] found limited empirical support in a 2003 population based study. The study found that the sequence was correct although acceptance was highest at all points throughout the person's experience.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1001/jama.297.7.716 |title=An Empirical Examination of the Stage Theory of Grief |year=2007 |last1=MacIejewski |first1=P. K. |last2=Zhang |first2=B. |last3=Block |first3=S. D. |last4=Prigerson |first4=H. G. |journal=JAMA: The Journal of the American Medical Association |volume=297 |issue=7 |pages=716–23 |pmid=17312291|doi-access=free }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Maciejewski |first1=Paul K. |last2=Zhang |first2=Baohui |last3=Block |first3=Susan D. |last4=Prigerson |first4=Holly G. |date=2007-02-21 |title=An Empirical Examination of the Stage Theory of Grief |journal=JAMA |volume=297 |issue=7 |pages=716–723 |doi=10.1001/jama.297.7.716 |pmid=17312291 |issn=0098-7484}}</ref>
paragraph: Adolescents may respond by [[Juvenile delinquency|delinquency]],<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hiyoshi|first1=A|last2=Berg|first2=L|last3=Grotta|first3=A|last4=Almquist|first4=Y|last5=Rostila|first5=M|date=1 September 2020|title=Childhood bereavement and adult mortality: A 65-year follow-up of the Stockholm birth cohort|journal=European Journal of Public Health|volume=30|issue=Supplement_5|article-number=ckaa165.051|doi=10.1093/eurpub/ckaa165.051|issn=1101-1262|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Feigelman|first1=William|last2=Rosen|first2=Zohn|last3=Joiner|first3=Thomas|last4=Silva|first4=Caroline|last5=Mueller|first5=Anna S.|date=16 March 2017|title=Examining longer-term effects of parental death in adolescents and young adults: Evidence from the National Longitudinal Survey of Adolescent to Adult Health|journal=Death Studies|volume=41|issue=3|pages=133–43|doi=10.1080/07481187.2016.1226990|issn=0748-1187|pmc=7219956|pmid=27813715}}</ref> or oppositely become "over-achievers". Repetitive actions are not uncommon such as washing a car repeatedly or taking up repetitive tasks such as sewing, computer games, etc. It is an effort to stay above the grief.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Maciejewski |first1=Paul K. |last2=Zhang |first2=Baohui |last3=Block |first3=Susan D. |last4=Prigerson |first4=Holly G. |date=2007-02-21 |title=An Empirical Examination of the Stage Theory of Grief |journal=JAMA |volume=297 |issue=7 |pages=716–723 |doi=10.1001/jama.297.7.716 |pmid=17312291 |issn=0098-7484}}</ref> Childhood loss can predispose a child not only to physical illness but to emotional problems and an increased risk for suicide, especially in the adolescent period.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cash|first1=Scottye J|last2=Bridge|first2=Jeffrey A|date=October 2009|title=Epidemiology of youth suicide and suicidal behavior|journal=Current Opinion in Pediatrics|volume=21|issue=5|pages=613–19|doi=10.1097/MOP.0b013e32833063e1|issn=1040-8703|pmc=2885157|pmid=19644372}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Kogut 2005a">{{cite journal| first1=Tehila| last1=Kogut| first2=Ilana| last2=Ritov| journal=Journal of Behavioral Decision Making| volume=18| number=3| pages=157–167| date=2005| url=https://pluto.mscc.huji.ac.il/~msiritov/KogutRitovIdentified.pdf| title=The "Identified Victim" Effect: An Identified Group, or Just a Single Individual?| doi=10.1002/bdm.492}}</ref>
paragraph: Studies have found that people respond more to concrete, graphic information than abstract, statistical information.<ref name= "Nisbett, 1980">{{Cite book|title=Human Inference: Strategies and Shortcomings of Social Judgment|last1=Nisbett|first1=Richard|last2=Ross|first2=Lee|publisher=Prentice-Hall|year=1980|isbn=978-0-13-445130-5|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/humaninferencest0000nisb}}</ref> Bohnet and Frey (1999)<ref>{{cite journal| date=1999| first1=Iris| last1=Bohnet| first2=Bruno S.| last2=Frey| title=Social distance and other-regarding behavior in dictator games: Comment| journal=The American Economic Review| volume=89| number=1| pages=335–339| doi=10.1257/aer.89.1.335| url=https://booksc.eu/book/61978738/97d32c| access-date=2021-07-15| archive-date=2021-12-14| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214140146/https://booksc.eu/book/61978738/97d32c| url-access=subscription}}</ref> and Kogut and Ritov (2005)<ref name="Kogut 2005a">{{cite journal| first1=Tehila| last1=Kogut| first2=Ilana| last2=Ritov| journal=Journal of Behavioral Decision Making| volume=18| number=3| pages=157–167| date=2005| url=https://pluto.mscc.huji.ac.il/~msiritov/KogutRitovIdentified.pdf| title=The "Identified Victim" Effect: An Identified Group, or Just a Single Individual?| doi=10.1002/bdm.492}}</ref> found that vividness contributes to the identifiable victim effect. Another study by Kogut and Ritov (2005)<ref name="Kogut 2005b">{{cite journal| first1=Tehila| last1=Kogut| first2=Ilana| last2=Ritov| journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes| volume=97| number=2| pages=106–116| date=2005| title=The singularity effect of identified victims in separate and joint evaluation| doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2005.02.003| url=https://www.academia.edu/15659171}}</ref> found that donations to benefit a needy child increased when the name and a picture of the child were provided. Jenni and Loewenstein (1997)<ref name= "Jenni & Loewenstein, 1997" /> did not observe an effect of vividness.

ref_block: <ref name= "Kogut, 2011">{{cite journal | last1 = Kogut | first1 = T | year = 2011 | title = Someone to blame: when identifying a victim decreases helping | url = http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com.proxy.library.nd.edu/store/10.1002/bdm.492/asset/492_ftp.pdf?v=1&t=j1vk6fsl&s=1a3860951722425fce0c1b7d8c04c57cc7ec8f93 | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 47 | issue = 4| pages = 748–755 | doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2011.02.011}}</ref>
paragraph:
=== When the victim can be blamed ===
Research suggests that if an individual is seen as responsible for their plight, people offer less help if the victim is identified.<ref name= "Kogut, 2011">{{cite journal | last1 = Kogut | first1 = T | year = 2011 | title = Someone to blame: when identifying a victim decreases helping | url = http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com.proxy.library.nd.edu/store/10.1002/bdm.492/asset/492_ftp.pdf?v=1&t=j1vk6fsl&s=1a3860951722425fce0c1b7d8c04c57cc7ec8f93 | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 47 | issue = 4| pages = 748–755 | doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2011.02.011}}</ref> Most research dedicated to the identifiable victim effect avoids the topic of blame, using explicitly blameless individuals, such as children suffering from an illness.<ref name= "Kogut & Ritov, 2005">{{cite journal | last1 = Kogut | first1 = T. | last2 = Ritov | first2 = I. | year = 2005 | title = The "identified victim" effect: an identified group, or just a single individual? | url = http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com.proxy.library.nd.edu/store/10.1002/bdm.492/asset/492_ftp.pdf?v=1&t=j1vk6fsl&s=1a3860951722425fce0c1b7d8c04c57cc7ec8f93 | journal = Journal of Behavioral Decision Making | volume = 18 | issue = 3| pages = 157–165 | doi=10.1002/bdm.492}}</ref> However, there are real-world situations where victims may be seen as to blame for their current situation. For example, in a 2011 study by Kogut, individuals were less likely to offer help to an [[HIV/AIDS|AIDS]] victim if the victim had contracted AIDS through sexual contact than if the individual was born with AIDS. In other words, individuals were less likely to offer help to victims if they were seen as at least partially responsible for their plight. A meta-study conducted in 2016 supports these findings, reporting that charitable donations were highest when the victim showed little responsibility for their victimization.<ref name= "2016 meta-analysis" />

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal| first1=David| last1=Fetherstonhaugh| first2=Paul| last2=Slovic| first3=Stephen| last3=Johnson| first4=Friedrich| last4=James| title=Insensitivity to the Value of Human Life: A Study of Psychophysical Numbing| journal=Journal of Risk and Uncertainty| volume=14| pages=283–300| date=May 1997| issue=3| doi=10.1023/A:1007744326393| s2cid=51033376}}</ref>
paragraph: Risk that is concentrated is perceived as greater than the same risk dispersed over a wider population. Identifiable victims are their own reference group; if they do not receive aid then the entire reference group will perish.<ref name= "Jenni & Loewenstein, 1997" /><ref>{{cite journal | title=Proportion dominance: The generality and variability of favoring relative savings over absolute savings| last=Bartels| first=Daniel M.| date=2006| journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes| volume=100| pages=76–95| doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2005.10.004}}</ref> For example, Fetherstonhaugh et al<ref>{{cite journal| first1=David| last1=Fetherstonhaugh| first2=Paul| last2=Slovic| first3=Stephen| last3=Johnson| first4=Friedrich| last4=James| title=Insensitivity to the Value of Human Life: A Study of Psychophysical Numbing| journal=Journal of Risk and Uncertainty| volume=14| pages=283–300| date=May 1997| issue=3| doi=10.1023/A:1007744326393| s2cid=51033376}}</ref> found that an intervention saving a fixed number of lives was considered less beneficial when more total lives were at risk.

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | first1=Deborah A.| last1=Small| first2=George| last2=Loewenstein| first3=Paul| last3=Slovic| journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes| volume=102| issue=2| date=March 2007| pages=143–153|title=Sympathy and callousness: The impact of deliberative thought on donations to identifiable and statistical victims"| doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2006.01.005}}</ref>
paragraph:
|last2=Shepard|first2=D|last3=Pliskin|first3=J|year=1980|title=The Economic Value of Changing Mortality Probabilities: A Decision-Theoretic Approach|journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics|volume=94 |issue=2|pages=373–396|doi=10.2307/1884546|jstor=1884546}} People may feel a responsibility to an actual identified victim{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-7B2338nHGgC&pg=PR1|title=Risk and Blame|last1=Douglas|first1=Professor Mary|last2=Douglas|first2=Professor of Anthropology Mary|date=2013-06-17|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-49004-0|language=en}} but not to a possible victim of a future tragedy that might not occur. This explanation is closest to what Thomas Schelling implied in his now-famous paper.

Jenni and Loewenstein (1997) did not find evidence that ''ex post'' vs ''ex ante'' evaluation contributes to the identifiable victim effect, but Small and Lowenstein (2003){{cite journal|first1=Deborah A.| last1=Small| first2=George| last2=Loewenstein| title=Helping a Victim or Helping the Victim: Altruism and Identifiability| journal=Journal of Risk and Uncertainty| volume=26| number=1| date=January 2003| pages=5–16| doi=10.1023/A:1022299422219| s2cid=207550600| url=https://creativescience.co/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/Helping-a-Victim-or-Helping-the-Victim.pdf}} did. Indeed, researchers have generally found that identified victims are more likely to be helped than statistical ones. For example, Small, Loewenstein, and Slovic{{cite journal | first1=Deborah A.| last1=Small| first2=George| last2=Loewenstein| first3=Paul| last3=Slovic| journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes| volume=102| issue=2| date=March 2007| pages=143–153|title=Sympathy and callousness: The impact of deliberative thought on donations to identifiable and statistical victims"| doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2006.01.005}} found that subjects donated much more money to help a single starving girl named Rokia than to relieve a famine described statistically.

ref_block: <ref name= "Gino, Shu, & Bazerman, 2010">{{cite journal | last1 = Gino | first1 = F. | last2 = Shu | first2 = L. L. | last3 = Bazerman | first3 = M. H. | year = 2010 | title = Nameless + harmless = blameless: When seemingly irrelevant factors influence judgment of (un)ethical behavior | journal = Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes | volume = 111 | issue = 2| pages = 93–101 | doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2009.11.001}}</ref>
paragraph: Other researchers suggested that outside observers, not only perpetrators, view unethical behavior as less unethical if the victim of the unethical behavior is unidentified.<ref name= "Gino, Shu, & Bazerman, 2010">{{cite journal | last1 = Gino | first1 = F. | last2 = Shu | first2 = L. L. | last3 = Bazerman | first3 = M. H. | year = 2010 | title = Nameless + harmless = blameless: When seemingly irrelevant factors influence judgment of (un)ethical behavior | journal = Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes | volume = 111 | issue = 2| pages = 93–101 | doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2009.11.001}}</ref> This could possibly result in less public outcry against unethical practices in a globalized business environment, where the victims are often unseen.

paragraph: In this regard, the theory expands on research that has illustrated the role of memory representations in reasoning processes,<ref name="Support theory: A nonextensional representation of subjective probability.">{{cite journal|last=Tversky|first=A.|author2=Koehler, D. J.|title=Support theory: A nonextensional representation of subjective probability.|journal=Psychological Review|year=1994|volume=101|issue=4|pages=547–567|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.101.4.547|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1066966}}</ref> the intersection of which has been previously underexplored.<ref name="Mindful judgment and decision making.">{{cite journal|last=Weber|first=E.U.|author2=Johnson, E. J.|s2cid=5560068|title=Mindful judgment and decision making.|journal=Annual Review of Psychology|year=2009|volume=60|pages=53–85|doi=10.1146/annurev.psych.60.110707.163633|pmid=18798706}}</ref> However, in certain circumstances, FTT predicts independence between memory and reasoning, specifically between reasoning tasks that rely on gist representations and memory tests that rely on verbatim representations. An example of this is research between the [[Framing effect (psychology)|risky choice framing task]] and [[working memory]], in which better working memory is not associated with a reduction in bias.<ref name="Memory reflected in our decisions: Higher working memory capacity predicts greater bias in risky choice.">{{cite journal|last=Corbin|first=J.C.|author2=McElroy, T. |author3=Black, C. |title=Memory reflected in our decisions: Higher working memory capacity predicts greater bias in risky choice.|journal=Judgment and Decision Making|year=2010|volume=5|issue=2|pages=110–115|doi=10.1017/S1930297500000966 |s2cid=13845219 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal |last=Amsterdam |first=B. |year=1972 |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |pmid=4679817 }}</ref>
paragraph:
==Evolved awareness in mirror==
In the earliest stages of development, infants are not aware that images in mirrors are themselves. Research was done on 88 children between 3 and 24 months.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Amsterdam |first=B. |year=1972 |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |pmid=4679817 }}</ref> Their behaviors were observed before a mirror. The results indicated that children's awareness of self-image followed three major age-related sequences:  
* From about 6 through 12 months of age, the first prolonged and repeated reaction of an infant to their mirror image is that of a sociable “playmate”. 
* In the second year of life, wariness and withdrawal appeared; self-admiring and embarrassed behavior accompanied those avoidance behaviors starting at 14 months, and was shown by 75% of the subjects after 20 months of age. 
* During the last part of the second year of life, from 20 to 24 months of age, 65% of the subjects demonstrated recognition of their mirror images.

paragraph:
{{Short description|Animal self-awareness test}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=December 2021}}
[[File:Mirror test with a Baboon.JPG|thumb|The [[hamadryas baboon]] is one of many primate species that has been administered the mirror test.|alt=]]
The '''mirror test'''—sometimes called the '''mark test''', '''mirror self-recognition''' ('''MSR''') '''test''', '''red spot technique''', or '''rouge test'''—is a [[animal behavior|behavioral]] technique developed in 1970 by American psychologist [[Gordon G. Gallup|Gordon Gallup Jr.]] to determine whether an animal possesses the ability of visual [[self-recognition]].<ref name="Gallup">{{cite journal
| author = Gallup, GG Jr.
| title = Chimpanzees: Self recognition
| journal = Science
| volume = 167
| pages = 86–87
| year = 1970
| doi = 10.1126/science.167.3914.86
| pmid = 4982211 
| issue = 3914 
| bibcode = 1970Sci...167...86G
| s2cid = 145295899
}}</ref>  In this test, an animal is [[anesthetized]] and then marked (e.g. paint or sticker) on an area of the body the animal normally cannot see (e.g. forehead). When the animal recovers from the anesthetic, it is given access to a [[mirror]]. If it subsequently touches or examines the mark on its own body, this behavior is interpreted as evidence that the animal recognizes its reflection as an image of itself, rather than another animal.

paragraph:
or-Induced-Behavior-in-the-Magpie-(Pica-pica)-Evidence-of-Self-Recognition-pbio.0060202.sv008.ogv|thumb|220px|Video of the responses of a European magpie in an MSR test: The magpie repeatedly attempts to remove the marks.]]
* [[Eurasian magpie]] (''Pica pica''): The Eurasian magpie is the first non-mammal to have been found to pass the mirror test. In 2008, researchers applied a small red, yellow, or black sticker to the throat of five Eurasian magpies, where they could be seen by the bird only by using a mirror. The birds were then given a mirror. The feel of the sticker on their throats did not seem to alarm the magpies. However, when the birds with colored stickers glimpsed themselves in the mirror, they scratched at their throats—a clear indication that they recognised the image in the mirror as their own. Those that received a black sticker, invisible against the black neck feathers, did not react.<ref name="Prior">{{cite journal
 |last1=Prior|first1=H.
 |last2=Schwarz|first2=A.
 |last3=Güntürkün|first3=O. 
 |title=Mirror-induced behavior in the magpie (''Pica pica''): Evidence of self-recognition 
 |journal=PLOS Biology 
 |year=2008 
 |doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.0060202 
 |doi-access=free
 |volume=6 
 |article-number=e202 
 |pmid=18715117 
 |issue=8 
 |pmc=2517622 
}}</ref> In 2020, researchers attempted to closely replicate the 2008 study with a larger number of magpies, and failed to confirm the results of the 2008 study. The researchers stated that while these results did not disprove the 2008 study, the failure to replicate indicated the results of the original study should be treated with caution.<ref name="Solar">{{cite journal

ref_block: <ref name=Amsterdam>{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |author=Beulah Amsterdam |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |year=1972 |pmid=4679817}}</ref>
paragraph:
==Humans==
[[File:Mirror baby.jpg|thumb|225px|A human child exploring his reflection]]
The rouge test is a version of the mirror test used with human children.<ref name=Amsterdam>{{cite journal |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |author=Beulah Amsterdam |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |year=1972 |pmid=4679817}}</ref> Using [[rouge (cosmetics)|rouge]] makeup, an experimenter surreptitiously places a dot on the face of the child. The children are then placed in front of a mirror and their reactions are monitored; depending on the child's development, distinct categories of responses are demonstrated. This test is widely cited as the primary measure for mirror self-recognition in human children.<ref name=Lewis>{{cite book |title=Social cognition and the acquisition of self |url=https://archive.org/details/socialcognitiona0000lewi |url-access=registration |last=Lewis |first=M. |author2=Brooks-Gunn, J. |year=1979 |publisher=Plenum Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-306-40232-6 |page= [https://archive.org/details/socialcognitiona0000lewi/page/296 296]}}</ref><ref name=Priel>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/0022-0965(86)90038-X |author=Priel, Beatrice |author2=de Schonen, Scania |title=Self-Recognition: A Study of a Population without Mirrors |journal=Journal of Experimental Child Psychology |volume=41 |issue=2 |pages=237–250 |year=1986 |pmid=3701250}}</ref><ref name=Sedikides>Sedikides, C. & Spencer, S.J. (Eds.) (2007). ''The Self''. New York: Psychology Press</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 | pmid=4679817 | volume=5 | issue=4 | title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two | year=1972 | journal=Dev Psychobiol | pages=297–305 | author=Amsterdam B}}</ref>
paragraph:
===Implications===
The rouge test is a measure of [[self-concept]]; the child who touches the rouge on their own nose upon looking into a mirror demonstrates the basic ability to understand self-awareness.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 | pmid=4679817 | volume=5 | issue=4 | title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two | year=1972 | journal=Dev Psychobiol | pages=297–305 | author=Amsterdam B}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7FK3AwAAQBAJ&q=Butterworth%2C+1992&pg=PA94|title=Self-awareness in the first few weeks of life|access-date=4 November 2017|isbn=978-1-136-87200-6|last1=Brown|first1=Jonathon|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Psychology Press }}</ref><ref>Social Psychology, 6th Edition p. 68-69</ref> Animals,<ref name="coren">{{cite book |title=How Dogs Think |author=Stanley Coren |isbn=978-0-7432-2232-7 |year=2004 |publisher=Free Press |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/howdogsthinkunde00core }}</ref> young children,<ref name="palmer">{{Cite web|url=https://www.ulm.edu/~palmer/ConsciousnessandtheSymbolicUniverse.htm|title=Consciousness and the Symbolic Universe|website=ulm.edu}}</ref> and people who have gained sight after being blind from birth,<ref name="archer">{{cite book |last=Archer |first=John |year=1992 |title=Ethology and Human Development|publisher=Rowman & Littlefield |isbn=978-0-389-20996-6}}</ref> sometimes react to their reflection in the mirror as though it were another individual.{{Citation needed|date=December 2014}}

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0022022110381114?journalCode=jcca | doi=10.1177/0022022110381114 | title=Cultural Variations in Children's Mirror Self-Recognition | date=2011 | last1=Broesch | first1=Tanya | last2=Callaghan | first2=Tara | last3=Henrich | first3=Joseph | last4=Murphy | first4=Christine | last5=Rochat | first5=Philippe | journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology | volume=42 | issue=6 | pages=1018–1029 | s2cid=18326326 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph: There is criticism that passing a rouge test may be culturally motivated, and that what is commonly thought about mirror self-recognition actually applies only to children of Western countries. A study from 2010 tested children from rural communities in Kenya, Fiji, Saint Lucia, Grenada and Peru, as well as urban United States and rural Canada. The majority of children from the US and Canada passed the MSR test, but fewer children from the other regions passed the MSR test. In the Kenya test, only 3% of children aged 18–72 months touched the mark. In the Fiji test, none of the children aged 36–55 months touched the mark. The other non-Western rural children scored much better, but still markedly worse than their Western counterparts.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0022022110381114?journalCode=jcca | doi=10.1177/0022022110381114 | title=Cultural Variations in Children's Mirror Self-Recognition | date=2011 | last1=Broesch | first1=Tanya | last2=Callaghan | first2=Tara | last3=Henrich | first3=Joseph | last4=Murphy | first4=Christine | last5=Rochat | first5=Philippe | journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology | volume=42 | issue=6 | pages=1018–1029 | s2cid=18326326 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0022022110381114?journalCode=jcca | doi=10.1177/0022022110381114 | title=Cultural Variations in Children's Mirror Self-Recognition | date=2011 | last1=Broesch | first1=Tanya | last2=Callaghan | first2=Tara | last3=Henrich | first3=Joseph | last4=Murphy | first4=Christine | last5=Rochat | first5=Philippe | journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology | volume=42 | issue=6 | pages=1018–1029 | s2cid=18326326 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph: There is criticism that passing a rouge test may be culturally motivated, and that what is commonly thought about mirror self-recognition actually applies only to children of Western countries. A study from 2010 tested children from rural communities in Kenya, Fiji, Saint Lucia, Grenada and Peru, as well as urban United States and rural Canada. The majority of children from the US and Canada passed the MSR test, but fewer children from the other regions passed the MSR test. In the Kenya test, only 3% of children aged 18–72 months touched the mark. In the Fiji test, none of the children aged 36–55 months touched the mark. The other non-Western rural children scored much better, but still markedly worse than their Western counterparts.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0022022110381114?journalCode=jcca | doi=10.1177/0022022110381114 | title=Cultural Variations in Children's Mirror Self-Recognition | date=2011 | last1=Broesch | first1=Tanya | last2=Callaghan | first2=Tara | last3=Henrich | first3=Joseph | last4=Murphy | first4=Christine | last5=Rochat | first5=Philippe | journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology | volume=42 | issue=6 | pages=1018–1029 | s2cid=18326326 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

paragraph: pages=434–444 |doi=10.1111/j.1532-7078.2009.00025.x |pmid=32693521 |issn=1532-7078|url-access=subscription }}</ref> However, they are generally thought to possess implicit mentalization<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Apperly |first1=Ian A |last2=Butterfill |first2=Stephen A |date=2009 |title=Do humans have two systems to track beliefs and belief-like states |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/a0016923 |journal=Psychological Review |volume=116 |issue=4 |pages=953–970 |doi=10.1037/a0016923 |issn=1939-1471 |pmid=19839692}}</ref>—fast, non-conscious, and non-verbal—rather than explicit mentalization, which is slower, conscious, verbal, and typically emerges around 4 years of age<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wimmer |first1=Heinz |last2=Perner |first2=Josef |date=1983 |title=Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children's understanding of deception |url=https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/0010-0277%2883%2990004-5 |journal=Cognition |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=103–128 |doi=10.1016/0010-0277(83)90004-5 |pmid=6681741 |issn=0010-0277}}</ref>. Implicit mentalization includes, for example, the ability to recognize when a communicative partner has incorrect or incomplete information about a relevant aspect of the world. Multiple independent studies suggest that, in such situations, infants communicate more frequently and accurately to share their knowledge with others<ref>{{Cite journal |last=O'Neill |first=Daniela K. |date=1996 |title=Two-Year-Old Children's Sensitivity to a Parent's Knowledge State When Making Requests |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8624.1996.tb01758.x |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=659–677 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.1996.tb01758.x |issn=0009-3920}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Knudsen |first1=Birgit |last2=Liszkowski |first2=Ulf |date=2012 |title=Eighteen- and 24-month-old infants correct others in anticipation of action mistakes |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-7687.2011.01098.x |journal=Developmental Science |language=en |volume=15 |issue=1 |pages=113–122 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-7687.2011.01098.x |pmid=22251297 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0013-408D-6 |issn=1363-755X}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tauzin |first1=Tibor |last2=Call |first2=Josep |last3=Gergely |first3=György |date=2024 |title=Infants Produce Optimally Informative Points to Satisfy the Epistemic Needs of Their Communicative Partner |journal=Open Mind |language=en |volume=8 |pages=1228–1246 |doi=10.1162/opmi_a_00166 |issn=2470-2986 |pmc=11520476 |pmid=39474157}}</ref>. paragraph:
==== Self-awareness ====
The most common technique used in research for testing self-awareness in infants is a mirror test known as the "[[Rouge Test]]".<ref name="Amsterdam">{{cite journal |last=Amsterdam |first=B. |year=1972 |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |pmid=4679817}}</ref><ref name="Gallup">{{cite journal |last=Gallup |first=G. G. |year=1970 |title=Chimpanzees: Self-recognition |journal=Science |volume=167 |issue=3914 |pages=86–87 |bibcode=1970Sci...167...86G |doi=10.1126/science.167.3914.86 |pmid=4982211 |s2cid=145295899}}</ref> The rouge test works by applying a dot on an infant's face and then placing them in front of the mirror. If the infant investigates the dot on their nose by touching it, they are thought to realize their own existence and have achieved self-awareness. A number of research studies have used this technique and shown self-awareness to develop between 15 and 24 months of age.<ref name="Lewis">{{cite book |last1=Lewis |first1=M. |url=https://archive.org/details/socialcognitiona0000lewi |title=Social cognition and the acquisition of self |last2=Brooks-Gunn |first2=J. |publisher=Plenum Press. |year=1979 |location=New York, NY |isbn=978-0-306-40232-6 |url-access=registration}}</ref><ref name="Rochat2">{{cite journal |last1=Rochat |first1=P. |last2=Broesch |first2=T. |last3=Jayne |first3=K. |year=2012 |title=Social awareness and early self-recognition |journal=Consciousness and Cognition |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=1491–1497 |doi=10.1016/j.concog.2012.04.007 |pmid=22673374 |s2cid=16697739}}</ref> Some researchers take language such as "I, me, my, etc." as an indicator of self-awareness.<ref name="Bates">{{cite book |last=Bates |first=E. |title=The self in transition: Infancy to childhood |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1990 |editor1-last=Cicchetti |editor1-first=D. |location=Chicago |pages=165–182 |chapter=Language about me and you: Pronominal reference and the emerging concept of self |editor2-last=Beeghly |editor2-first=M.}}</ref>

== Autism ==
{{See also|Autism rights movement#Ethical challenges to autism treatment}}
Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) are known to have restricted or repetitive behaviors that can cause problems when trying to function in day-to-day activities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rispoli |first1=Mandy |last2=Camargo |first2=Siglia |last3=Machalicek |first3=Wendy |last4=Lang |first4=Russell |last5=Sigafoos |first5=Jeff |date=2014 |title=Functional communication training in the treatment of problem behavior maintained by access to rituals |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/jaba.130 |journal=Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis |volume=27 |issue=47 |page=3 |doi=10.1002/jaba.1994.27.issue-1 |issn=0021-8855|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Some problem behaviors may include, but are not limited to, self-injurious behaviors, aggression, tantrums, problems with sleep, and making choices.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hanley |first1=Gregory P. |last2=Jin |first2=C. == Autism ==
{{See also|Autism rights movement#Ethical challenges to autism treatment}}
Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) are known to have restricted or repetitive behaviors that can cause problems when trying to function in day-to-day activities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rispoli |first1=Mandy |last2=Camargo |first2=Siglia |last3=Machalicek |first3=Wendy |last4=Lang |first4=Russell |last5=Sigafoos |first5=Jeff |date=2014 |title=Functional communication training in the treatment of problem behavior maintained by access to rituals |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/jaba.130 |journal=Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis |volume=27 |issue=47 |page=3 |doi=10.1002/jaba.1994.27.issue-1 |issn=0021-8855|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Some problem behaviors may include, but are not limited to, self-injurious behaviors, aggression, tantrums, problems with sleep, and making choices.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hanley |first1=Gregory P. |last2=Jin |first2=C. Sandy |last3=Vanselow |first3=Nicholas R. |last4=Hanratty |first4=Laura A. |date=2014 |title=Producing meaningful improvements in problem behavior of children with autism via synthesized analyses and treatments: Severe Problem Behavior |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jaba.106 |journal=Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis |language=en |volume=47 |issue=1 |pages=16–36 |doi=10.1002/jaba.106 |pmid=24615474|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Extinction is often used as an intervention to help with problem behaviors.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Falcomata |first1=Terry S. |last2=Hoffman |first2=Katherine J. |last3=Gainey |first3=Summer |last4=Muething |first4=Colin S. |last5=Fienup |first5=Daniel M. |date=2013-07-10 |title=A Preliminary Evaluation of Reinstatement of Destructive Behavior Displayed by Individuals With Autism |url=http://thepsychologicalrecord.siu.edu/Summer_2013.html |journal=The Psychological Record |volume=63 |issue=3 |pages=453–466 |doi=10.11133/j.tpr.2013.63.3.004 |doi-broken-date=12 July 2025 |s2cid=147662158|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

paragraph:
==== Corporate political activity ====
CSA is distinct from CPA—a firm's efforts (e.g. campaign contributions, [[lobbying]], and donations to [[political action committee]]s) to sway political processes and gain policy-related market advantages.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lux|first1=Sean|last2=Crook|first2=T. Russell|last3=Woehr|first3=David J.|date=2010-12-20|title=Mixing Business With Politics: A Meta-Analysis of the Antecedents and Outcomes of Corporate Political Activity|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0149206310392233|journal=Journal of Management|volume=37|pages=223–247|language=en|doi=10.1177/0149206310392233|s2cid=144560276|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hillman|first1=Amy J.|last2=Keim|first2=Gerald D.|last3=Schuler|first3=Douglas|date=2016-06-23|title=Corporate Political Activity: A Review and Research Agenda|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1016/j.jm.2004.06.003|journal=Journal of Management|volume=30|issue=6|pages=837–857|language=en|doi=10.1016/j.jm.2004.06.003|s2cid=153503017|url-access=subscription}}</ref> While both types of firm activities reflect involvement in the political process, they differ in the extent to which they are publicized. CSA is often utilized as a public demonstration of a firm's core values and principles. Conversely, CPA is an often-discreet activity that is typically made public only through "accidental disclosure".<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Werner|first=Timothy|date=2017|title=Investor Reaction to Covert Corporate Political Activity|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/smj.2682|journal=Strategic Management Journal|language=en|volume=38|issue=12|pages=2424–2443|doi=10.1002/smj.2682|issn=1097-0266|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="ChowdhuryJeon2017">{{cite journal|last1=Chowdhury|first1=Subhasish M.|last2=Jeon|first2=Joo Young|last3=Saha|first3=Bibhas|title=Gender Differences in the Giving and Taking Variants of the Dictator Game|journal=Southern Economic Journal|volume=84|issue=2|year=2017|pages=474–483|issn=0038-4038|doi=10.1002/soej.12223|s2cid=146250008 |url=http://centaur.reading.ac.uk/73401/1/DGTG.pdf}}</ref>
paragraph: A number of studies have examined [[Framing effect (psychology)|psychological framing]] of the dictator game with a version called "taking" in which the player "takes" resources from the recipient's predetermined endowment, rather than choosing the amount to "give".<ref name="AlevyJeffries2014">{{cite journal|last1=Alevy|first1=Jonathan E.|last2=Jeffries|first2=Francis L.|last3=Lu|first3=Yonggang|title=Gender- and frame-specific audience effects in dictator games|journal=Economics Letters|volume=122|issue=1|year=2014|pages=50–54|issn=0165-1765|doi=10.1016/j.econlet.2013.10.030|url=http://www.econpapers.uaa.alaska.edu/RePEC/ala/wpaper/ALA201302.pdf}}</ref><ref name="ZhangOrtmann2013">{{cite journal|last1=Zhang|first1=Le|last2=Ortmann|first2=Andreas|title=The effects of the take-option in dictator-game experiments: a comment on Engel's (2011) meta-study|journal=Experimental Economics|volume=17|issue=3|year=2013|pages=414–420|issn=1386-4157|doi=10.1007/s10683-013-9375-7|s2cid=144098452}}</ref> Some studies show no effect between male and female players, but one 2017 study reported a difference between male and female players in the taking frame, with females allocating significantly more to the recipient under the "taking" frame compared to the "giving" frame, while males showed exactly the opposite behavior – nullifying the overall effect.<ref name="ChowdhuryJeon2017">{{cite journal|last1=Chowdhury|first1=Subhasish M.|last2=Jeon|first2=Joo Young|last3=Saha|first3=Bibhas|title=Gender Differences in the Giving and Taking Variants of the Dictator Game|journal=Southern Economic Journal|volume=84|issue=2|year=2017|pages=474–483|issn=0038-4038|doi=10.1002/soej.12223|s2cid=146250008 |url=http://centaur.reading.ac.uk/73401/1/DGTG.pdf}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Archer |first1=Louise |last2=Dawson |first2=Emily |last3=DeWitt |first3=Jennifer |last4=Seakins |first4=Amy |last5=Wong |first5=Billy |date=2015 |title="Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts |url=http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf |journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching |volume=52 |issue=7 |pages=922–948 |bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A |doi=10.1002/tea.21227 |s2cid=142013766 |archive-date=24 July 2018 |access-date=2 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180724191751/http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref>
paragraph:
==== Science capital ====
In the UK, [[Louise Archer (sociologist)|Louise Archer]] and colleagues (2015) developed the concept of ''[[science capital]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Archer |first1=Louise |last2=Dawson |first2=Emily |last3=DeWitt |first3=Jennifer |last4=Seakins |first4=Amy |last5=Wong |first5=Billy |date=2015 |title="Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts |url=http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf |journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching |volume=52 |issue=7 |pages=922–948 |bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A |doi=10.1002/tea.21227 |s2cid=142013766 |archive-date=24 July 2018 |access-date=2 September 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180724191751/http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> The concept of science capital draws from the work of Bourdieu, particularly his studies focusing on the reproduction of social inequalities in society. Science capital is made up of science-related cultural capital and [[social capital]] as well as [[Habitus (sociology)|habitus]]. It encapsulates the various influences that a young person's life experiences can have on their science identity and participation in science-related activities. The empirical work on science capital builds from a growing body of data into students' aspirations and attitudes to science, including [[University College London]]'s ASPIRES Research<ref>[https://www.ucl.ac.uk/ioe/departments-centres/departments/education-practice-and-society/aspires/ ASPIRES Research] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170818131443/http://www.ucl.ac.uk/ioe/departments-centres/departments/education-practice-and-society/aspires |date=18 August 2017 }}. University College, London. 2020.</ref> and [[King's College London]]'s Enterprising Science.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Enterprising Science |url=http://www.kcl.ac.uk/sspp/departments/education/research/Research-Centres/cppr/Research/currentpro/Enterprising-Science/index.aspx |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170510014059/http://www.kcl.ac.uk/sspp/departments/education/research/Research-Centres/cppr/Research/currentpro/Enterprising-Science/index.aspx |archive-date=10 May 2017 |access-date=14 May 2017}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Archer |first1=Louise |last2=Dawson |first2=Emily |last3=DeWitt |first3=Jennifer |last4=Seakins |first4=Amy |last5=Wong |first5=Billy |title=''Science Capital'': A Conceptual, Methodological, and Empirical Argument for Extending Bourdieusian Notions of Capital Beyond the Arts |journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching |date=2015 |volume=52 |issue=7 |page=992-948 |doi=10.1002/tea.21227 |bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A |doi-access=free }}</ref>
paragraph:
{{Short description|Conceptual tool in sociology of education}}
'''Science capital''' is a conceptual tool developed by [[Louise Archer (academic)|Louise Archer]] and colleagues at [[King's College London]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Archer |first1=Louise |last2=Dawson |first2=Emily |last3=DeWitt |first3=Jennifer |last4=Seakins |first4=Amy |last5=Wong |first5=Billy |title=''Science Capital'': A Conceptual, Methodological, and Empirical Argument for Extending Bourdieusian Notions of Capital Beyond the Arts |journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching |date=2015 |volume=52 |issue=7 |page=992-948 |doi=10.1002/tea.21227 |bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A |doi-access=free }}</ref> It uses the theoretical frameworks created by French sociologist [[Pierre Bourdieu]] to summarise an individual’s science-related [[habitus (sociology)|habitus]] and [[cultural capital|capital]]. It can be used to help understanding how [[social class]] affects people's aspirations and involvement in science. The concept comes from research in education but is also used more broadly in practice and policy, for instance in the work of the [[Science and Technology Select Committee|Science and Technology Committee]] of the House of Commons in the UK.<ref name = HoC>{{Cite web|url=https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/cm201617/cmselect/cmsctech/162/162.pdf|title=House of Commons Science and Technology Committee Science communication and engagement|last=House of Commons|access-date=8 May 2017}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Dawson|first2=Emily|last3=DeWitt|first3=Jen|last4=Seakins|first4=Amy|last5=Wong|first5=Billy|date=2015|title=Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts|url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=52|issue=7|pages=922–948|doi=10.1002/tea.21227|bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A|doi-access=free}}</ref>
paragraph: Science capital is made up of science related [[Cultural capital|cultural]] and [[social capital]] (institutionalized and/or embodied through knowledge, consumption, credentials, and social networks) as well as [[Habitus (sociology)|habitus]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Dawson|first2=Emily|last3=DeWitt|first3=Jen|last4=Seakins|first4=Amy|last5=Wong|first5=Billy|date=2015|title=Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts|url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=52|issue=7|pages=922–948|doi=10.1002/tea.21227|bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=DeWitt|first2=Jen|last3=Willis|first3=Beatrice|date=2013|title=Adolescent boys' science aspirations: Masculinity, capital, and power|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=51 |issue=1|pages=1–30|doi=10.1002/tea.21122}}</ref> Researchers have suggested that science capital does not exist in isolation but has its value determined by someone's wider context and environment.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Dawson|first2=Emily|last3=DeWitt|first3=Jen|last4=Seakins|first4=Amy|last5=Wong|first5=Billy|date=2015|title="Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts|url=https://centaur.reading.ac.uk/69983/1/Archer_et_al-2015-Journal_of_Research_in_Science_Teaching.pdf|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=52|issue=7|pages=922–948|doi=10.1002/tea.21227|bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A|doi-access=free}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name=":6">{{cite journal|title = Goals as reference points|last = Heath, C., Larrick, R. P., & Wu, G|date = 1999|journal = Cognitive Psychology  |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=79–109 |doi = 10.1006/cogp.1998.0708|pmid = 10090799|citeseerx = 10.1.1.474.3054|s2cid = 6796370}}</ref>
paragraph:
==== Prospect Theory Perspective ====
Heath, Larrick, and Wu (1999) assert that motivation to pursue a goal can be explained using the principles of [[Prospect theory]] – specifically, those associated with the S-shaped value function.<ref name=":6" />  This values approach to motivation emphasizes the following characteristics:
* reference points,
* [[loss aversion]], and
* diminishing sensitivity.<ref name=":6">{{cite journal|title = Goals as reference points|last = Heath, C., Larrick, R. P., & Wu, G|date = 1999|journal = Cognitive Psychology  |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=79–109 |doi = 10.1006/cogp.1998.0708|pmid = 10090799|citeseerx = 10.1.1.474.3054|s2cid = 6796370}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Malle and Knobe">{{cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F. |last2=Knobe |first2=Joshua |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=March 1997 |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314 |s2cid=14173135 }}</ref>
paragraph: [[Folk psychology]] explains human behavior on the basis of mental states, including [[beliefs]], [[desires]], and intentions.<ref name="Astington">{{cite book|last1=Astington|first1=J.W.|title=The child's discovery of the mind|date=1993|publisher=Harvard University Press|location=Cambridge, Massachusetts}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Perner|first1=J.|title=Understanding the representational mind|date=1991|publisher=Bradford Books/MIT Press|location=Cambridge, Massachusetts}}</ref> This explanation is based on the idea that desires motivate behavior and beliefs direct the behavior towards the desired [[goal]].<ref name="Malle and Knobe">{{cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F. |last2=Knobe |first2=Joshua |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=March 1997 |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314 |s2cid=14173135 }}</ref> This can be understood in terms of causal chains, i.e. that desires cause intentions, intentions cause actions, and actions cause the realization of the desired outcome.<ref name="Astington" />

ref_block: <ref name="Malle 1997 101–121">{{cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F. |last2=Knobe |first2=Joshua |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=March 1997 |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314 }}</ref>
paragraph:
==Key folk concepts==
===Intentionality===
When perceiving, explaining, or criticizing [[human behaviour]], people distinguish between [[Volition (psychology)|intentional and unintentional action]]s.<ref name="Malle 1997 101–121">{{cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F. |last2=Knobe |first2=Joshua |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=March 1997 |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314 }}</ref> An evaluation of an action as stemming from purposeful action or accidental circumstances is one of the key determinants in social interaction. Others are the environmental conditions or pre-cognitive matters. For example, a critical remark that is judged to be intentional on the part of the receiver of the message can be viewed as a hurtful insult. Conversely, if considered unintentional, the same remark may be dismissed and forgiven.

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Malle|first1=Bertram F.|last2=Knobe|first2=Joshua|s2cid=14173135|date=1997|title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|volume=33|issue=2|pages=101–121|doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314|issn=0022-1031}}</ref>
paragraph: Bertram Malle and [[Joshua Knobe]] conducted survey studies to investigate laypeople's understanding and use (the folk concept) of the word 'intentionality' and its relation to action.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Malle|first1=Bertram F.|last2=Knobe|first2=Joshua|s2cid=14173135|date=1997|title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|volume=33|issue=2|pages=101–121|doi=10.1006/jesp.1996.1314|issn=0022-1031}}</ref> His data suggested that people think of [[intentionality]] of an action in terms of several psychological constituents: desire for outcome, belief about the expected outcome, intention to act (combination of desire and belief), skill to bring about the outcome, and awareness of action while performing that action. Consistent with this view as well as with our moral intuitions, studies found significant effects of the agent's intention, desire, and beliefs on various types of moral judgments, Using factorial designs to manipulate the content in the scenarios, Cushman showed that the agent's belief and desire regarding a harmful action significantly influenced judgments of wrongness, permissibility, punishment, and blame. However, whether the action actually brought about negative consequence or not only affected blame and punishment judgments, but not wrongness and permissibility judgments.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Cushman|first=Fiery|date=2008|title=Crime and punishment: Distinguishing the roles of causal and intentional analyses in moral judgment|journal=Cognition|volume=108|issue=2|pages=353–380|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2008.03.006|pmid=18439575|s2cid=2193343|issn=0010-0277}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Malle|first1=Bertram F.|last2=Guglielmo|first2=Steve|last3=Monroe|first3=Andrew E.|date=2014-04-03|title=A Theory of Blame|journal=Psychological Inquiry|volume=25|issue=2|pages=147–186|doi=10.1080/1047840x.2014.877340|issn=1047-840X|hdl=2027.42/147150|s2cid=10477084|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Another study also provided neuroscientific evidence for the interplay between [[theory of mind]] and moral judgment.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Young|first1=L.|last2=Cushman|first2=F.|last3=Hauser|first3=M.|last4=Saxe|first4=R.|date=2007-05-07|title=The neural basis of the interaction between theory of mind and moral judgment|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|volume=104|issue=20|pages=8235–8240|doi=10.1073/pnas.07

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal|last=Knobe|first=Joshua|date=2003|title=Intentional action in folk psychology: An experimental investigation|journal=Philosophical Psychology|volume=16|issue=2|pages=309–324|doi=10.1080/09515080307771|s2cid=12326690|issn=0951-5089|url=http://cogprints.org/2922/1/IntentionSkill.pdf}}</ref>
paragraph: Through another set of studies, Knobe showed a significant effect in the opposite direction: Intentionality judgments are significantly affected by the reasoner's moral evaluation of the actor and action.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Knobe|first=J.|date=2003|title=Intentional action and side effects in ordinary language|journal=Analysis|volume=63|issue=3|pages=190–194|doi=10.1093/analys/63.3.190|issn=0003-2638|url=http://cogprints.org/3116/2/IntentionalAction.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Knobe|first=Joshua|date=2003|title=Intentional action in folk psychology: An experimental investigation|journal=Philosophical Psychology|volume=16|issue=2|pages=309–324|doi=10.1080/09515080307771|s2cid=12326690|issn=0951-5089|url=http://cogprints.org/2922/1/IntentionSkill.pdf}}</ref> In one of his scenarios, a CEO of a corporation hears about a new programme designed to increase profit. However, the program is also expected to benefit or harm the environment as a side effect, to which he responds by saying 'I don't care'. The side effect was judged as intentional by the majority of participants in the harm condition, but the response pattern was reversed in the benefit condition.

<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Helzer|first1=Erik G.|last2=Pizarro|first2=David A.|s2cid=18764776|date=2011-03-18|title=Dirty Liberals!: Reminders of Physical Cleanliness Influence Moral and Political Attitudes|journal=Psychological Science|volume=22|issue=4|pages=517–522|doi=10.1177/0956797611402514|pmid=21421934|issn=0956-7976}}</ref>
paragraph: A notable illustration of the influence of intuition involved feeling of disgust. According to Haidt's [[moral foundations theory]],  political liberals rely on two dimensions (harm/care and fairness/reciprocity) of evaluation to make moral judgments, but conservatives utilize three additional dimensions (ingroup/loyalty, authority/respect, and purity/sanctity).<ref name=":6" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Graham|first1=Jesse|last2=Haidt|first2=Jonathan|last3=Nosek|first3=Brian A.|date=2009|title=Liberals and conservatives rely on different sets of moral foundations.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=96|issue=5|pages=1029–1046|doi=10.1037/a0015141|pmid=19379034|s2cid=2715121 |issn=1939-1315}}</ref> Among these, studies have revealed the link between moral evaluations based on purity/sanctity dimension and reasoner's experience of disgust. That is, people with higher sensitivity to disgust were more likely to be conservative toward political issues such as gay marriage and abortion.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Inbar|first1=Yoel|last2=Pizarro|first2=David A.|last3=Bloom|first3=Paul|date=2009|title=Conservatives are more easily disgusted than liberals|journal=Cognition & Emotion|volume=23|issue=4|pages=714–725|doi=10.1080/02699930802110007|s2cid=7411404|issn=0269-9931|citeseerx=10.1.1.372.3053}}</ref> Moreover, when the researchers reminded participants of keeping the lab clean and washing their hands with antiseptics (thereby priming the purity/sanctity dimension), participants' attitudes were more conservative than in the control condition.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Helzer|first1=Erik G.|last2=Pizarro|first2=David A.|s2cid=18764776|date=2011-03-18|title=Dirty Liberals!: Reminders of Physical Cleanliness Influence Moral and Political Attitudes|journal=Psychological Science|volume=22|issue=4|pages=517–522|doi=10.1177/0956797611402514|pmid=21421934|issn=0956-7976}}</ref> In turn, Helzer and Pizarro's findings have been rebutted by two failed attempts of replications.<ref>{{cite journal | pmid=32551744 | year=2020 | last1=Burnham | first1=B. ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F.| last2=Knobe | first2=Joshua |date= March 1997 |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi= 10.1006/jesp.1996.1314}}</ref>
paragraph:
==Implications==
Research into intentionality bias suggested that humans are inclined towards intentional judgements, which had significant implications for the understanding of everyday situations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rosset |first1=Evelyn | last2=Rottman | first2=Joshua |date= January 2014 |title=The Big 'Whoops!' in the Study of Intentional Behavior: An Appeal for a New Framework in Understanding Human Actions |journal=Journal of Cognition and Culture |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1–2 |pages=27–39 |doi= 10.1163/15685373-12342108}}</ref> For instance, intentionality bias was associated with '''aggressive behaviour''', and aggressive children were more likely to attribute hostile intentions to a peer.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=De Castro |first1=Bram Orobio | last2=Veerman | first2=Jan W.| last3=Koops | first3=Willem | last4=Bosch | first4=Joop D.| last5=Monshouwer | first5=Heidi J. |date= June 2002 |title=Hostile Attribution of Intent and Aggressive Behavior: A Meta-Analysis |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=73 |issue=3 |pages=916–934 |doi= 10.1111/1467-8624.00447 |pmid=12038560 }}</ref> Moreover, intentional inferences are often accompanied by '''accountability''' attribution, and individuals were more motivated to blame intentional harm-doers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F.| last2=Knobe | first2=Joshua |date= March 1997 |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi= 10.1006/jesp.1996.1314}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ames |first1=Daniel L.| last2=Fiske | first2=Susan T. |date= March 2015 |title=Perceived intent motivates people to magnify observed harms |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=112 |issue=12 |pages=3599–3605 |doi= 10.1073/pnas.1501592112 |doi-access=free |pmid=25733850 |pmc=4378403 |bibcode=2015PNAS..112.3599A }}</ref>

==Implications==
Research into intentionality bias suggested that humans are inclined towards intentional judgements, which had significant implications for the understanding of everyday situations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rosset |first1=Evelyn | last2=Rottman | first2=Joshua |date= January 2014 |title=The Big 'Whoops!' in the Study of Intentional Behavior: An Appeal for a New Framework in Understanding Human Actions |journal=Journal of Cognition and Culture |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1–2 |pages=27–39 |doi= 10.1163/15685373-12342108}}</ref> For instance, intentionality bias was associated with '''aggressive behaviour''', and aggressive children were more likely to attribute hostile intentions to a peer.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=De Castro |first1=Bram Orobio | last2=Veerman | first2=Jan W.| last3=Koops | first3=Willem | last4=Bosch | first4=Joop D.| last5=Monshouwer | first5=Heidi J. |date= June 2002 |title=Hostile Attribution of Intent and Aggressive Behavior: A Meta-Analysis |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=73 |issue=3 |pages=916–934 |doi= 10.1111/1467-8624.00447 |pmid=12038560 }}</ref> Moreover, intentional inferences are often accompanied by '''accountability''' attribution, and individuals were more motivated to blame intentional harm-doers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F.| last2=Knobe | first2=Joshua |date= March 1997 |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi= 10.1006/jesp.1996.1314}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ames |first1=Daniel L.| last2=Fiske | first2=Susan T. |date= March 2015 |title=Perceived intent motivates people to magnify observed harms |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=112 |issue=12 |pages=3599–3605 |doi= 10.1073/pnas.1501592112 |doi-access=free |pmid=25733850 |pmc=4378403 |bibcode=2015PNAS..112.3599A }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Custance D, Mayer J |date=September 2012 |title=Empathic-like responding by domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) to distress in humans: an exploratory study |url=https://research.gold.ac.uk/id/eprint/7074/2/Custance_and_Mayer_draft_prior_to_publication.pdf |journal=Animal Cognition |volume=15 |issue=5 |pages=851–9 |doi=10.1007/s10071-012-0510-1 |pmid=22644113 |s2cid=15153091}}</ref>
paragraph: [[Dog]]s have been hypothesized to share empathic-like responding towards humans. Researchers Custance and Mayer put individual dogs in an enclosure with their owner and a stranger.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Custance D, Mayer J |date=September 2012 |title=Empathic-like responding by domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) to distress in humans: an exploratory study |url=https://research.gold.ac.uk/id/eprint/7074/2/Custance_and_Mayer_draft_prior_to_publication.pdf |journal=Animal Cognition |volume=15 |issue=5 |pages=851–9 |doi=10.1007/s10071-012-0510-1 |pmid=22644113 |s2cid=15153091}}</ref> When the participants were talking or humming, the dog showed no behavioral changes; however when the participants were pretending to cry, the dogs oriented their behavior toward the person in distress whether it be the owner or stranger. The dogs approached the participants when crying in a submissive fashion, by sniffing, licking, and nuzzling the distressed person. The dogs did not approach the participants in the usual form of excitement, tail wagging, or panting. Since the dogs did not direct their empathic-like responses only towards their owner, it is hypothesized that dogs generally seek out humans showing distressing body behavior. Although this could suggest that dogs have the cognitive capacity for empathy, it could also mean that domesticated dogs have learned to comfort distressed humans through generations of being rewarded for that specific behavior.

paragraph: '''''Psychometric Development:''''' Meston has developed a number of questionnaires and psychometric tools to measure constructs in sexuality ranging from sexual function to sexual satisfaction. She co-authored the Female Sexual Function Index,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Rosen, C. Brown, J. Heiman, S. Leib|first=R.|date=April 2000|title=The Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI): A Multidimensional Self-Report Instrument for the Assessment of Female Sexual Function|journal=Journal of Sex & Marital Therapy|language=en|volume=26|issue=2|pages=191–208|doi=10.1080/009262300278597|pmid=10782451|s2cid=216086942 |issn=0092-623X|doi-access=free}}</ref> which is the most widely used psychometric measure of female sexual function. She also authored the ''Sexual Satisfaction Scale for Women'',<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy|last2=Trapnell|first2=Paul|date=January 2005|title=Development and Validation of a Five-Factor Sexual Satisfaction and Distress Scale for Women: The Sexual Satisfaction Scale for Women (SSS-W)|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=2|issue=1|pages=66–81|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2005.20107.x|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2859306|pmid=16422909}}</ref> the ''Why Have Sex Questionnaire'',<ref name=":6" /> and the ''Cues for Sexual Desire Sca''le.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=McCall|first1=Katie|last2=Meston|first2=Cindy|date=September 2006|title=Cues Resulting in Desire for Sexual Activity in Women|journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|volume=3|issue=5|pages=838–852|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2006.00301.x|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2861288|pmid=16942529}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Buss|first2=David M.|date=2007-08-01|title=Why Humans Have Sex|url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=36|issue=4|pages=477–507|doi=10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|pmid=17610060|s2cid=6182053|issn=1573-2800|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
paragraph: '''''Sexual Motivation:''''' Few researchers have historically examined sexual motives in women, assuming such motives were likely self-explanatory: for pleasure, for procreation, for love. Together with evolutionary psychologist David Buss, Meston questioned the simplicity of these assumptions and conducted a large-scale study of sexual motives, finding that women actually report 237 distinct reasons for having sex that range from experience-seeking to mate-guarding.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Buss|first2=David M.|date=2007-08-01|title=Why Humans Have Sex|url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=36|issue=4|pages=477–507|doi=10.1007/s10508-007-9175-2|pmid=17610060|s2cid=6182053|issn=1573-2800|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Meston has also conducted a series of follow-up studies finding that such motives vary based on sexual satisfaction levels <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stephenson|first1=Kyle R.|last2=Ahrold|first2=Tierney K.|last3=Meston|first3=Cindy M.|date=2011-06-01|title=The Association Between Sexual Motives and Sexual Satisfaction: Gender Differences and Categorical Comparisons|url= |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior|language=en|volume=40|issue=3|pages=607–618|doi=10.1007/s10508-010-9674-4|issn=1573-2800|pmc=3038192|pmid=20967494}}</ref> and age.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Meston|first1=Cindy M.|last2=Hamilton|first2=Lisa Dawn|last3=Harte|first3=Christopher B.|date=2009-12-01|title=Sexual Motivation in Women as a Function of Age|url= |journal=The Journal of Sexual Medicine|language=en|volume=6|issue=12|pages=3305–3319|doi=10.1111/j.1743-6109.2009.01489.x|pmid=19751384|issn=1743-6095|pmc=2978963}}</ref>

paragraph: People engage in sexual activity for any of a multitude of possible reasons. paragraph:
=== Influence on research ===
Many philosophers have argued that consciousness is a unitary concept that is understood by the majority of people despite the difficulty philosophers have had defining it.<ref name="Antony2001">{{cite journal|author=Michael V. Antony|year=2001|title=Is ''consciousness'' ambiguous?|journal=Journal of Consciousness Studies|volume=8|pages=19–44}}</ref> The term 'subjective experience', following Nagel, is ambiguous, as philosophers seem to differ from non-philosophers in their intuitions about its meaning.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Justin Sytsma |author2=Edouard Machery |title=Two conceptions of subjective experience |journal=Philosophical Studies |year=2010 |volume=151 |issue=2 |pages=299–327 |doi=10.1007/s11098-009-9439-x|s2cid=2444730 |url=http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221009/http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf |archive-date=2022-10-09 |url-status=live}}</ref> [[Max Velmans]] proposed that the "everyday understanding of consciousness" uncontroversially "refers to experience itself rather than any particular thing that we observe or experience" and he added that consciousness "is [therefore] exemplified by {{em|all}} the things that we observe or experience",{{r|Velmans2009|p=4}}<!--COMMENT: Velman's statement is a confusion of logical categories, an error of logical typing. Empirical science has discovered much about the processes of perception because there are 'objects' perceived by 'organs' of perception; but "experience itself" is an idea, a concept, an abstraction. Since 'experience' is not some 'thing' experienced, therefore (by definition) it cannot be empirically analyzed, reduced or compared. The 'things that we actually observe or experience' are examples of 'things' and 'kinds of things'; it's incorrect to call them 'examples of experience'! --> whether thoughts, feelings, or perceptions. [[Max Velmans|Velmans]] noted however, as of 2009, that there was a deep level of "confusion and internal division"<ref name=Velmans2009>{{cite journal|author=Max Velmans|title=How to define consciousness—and how not to define consciousness|journal=Journal of Consciousness Studies|year=2009|volume=16|pages=139–156|author-link=Max Velmans}}</ref> among experts about the phenomenon of consciousness, because researchers lacked "a sufficiently well-specified use of the term...to agree that they are investigating the same thing".{{r|Velmans2009|p=3}} He argued additionally that "pre-existing theoretical commitments" to competing explanations of consciousness might be a source of bias.{{cn|date=M

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal|author=Justin Sytsma|author2=Edouard Machery|title=Two conceptions of subjective experience|journal=Philosophical Studies|year=2010|volume=151|issue=2|pages=299–327|doi=10.1007/s11098-009-9439-x|s2cid=2444730|url=http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221009/http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf|archive-date=2022-10-09|url-status=live}}</ref>
paragraph:
=== Coherence of the concept ===
Philosophers differ from non-philosophers in their intuitions about what consciousness is.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Justin Sytsma|author2=Edouard Machery|title=Two conceptions of subjective experience|journal=Philosophical Studies|year=2010|volume=151|issue=2|pages=299–327|doi=10.1007/s11098-009-9439-x|s2cid=2444730|url=http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221009/http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/archive/00004888/01/Two_Conceptions_of_Subjective_Experience.pdf|archive-date=2022-10-09|url-status=live}}</ref> While most people have a strong intuition for the existence of what they refer to as consciousness,<ref name=Antony2001/> skeptics argue that this intuition is too narrow, either because the concept of consciousness is embedded in our intuitions, or because we all are illusions. [[Gilbert Ryle]], for example, argued that traditional understanding of consciousness depends on a [[mind–body dualism|Cartesian dualist]] outlook that improperly distinguishes between mind and body, or between mind and world. He proposed that we speak not of minds, bodies, and the world, but of entities, or identities, acting in the world. Thus, by speaking of "consciousness" we end up leading ourselves by thinking that there is any sort of thing as consciousness separated from behavioral and linguistic understandings.<ref name=RyleConsciousness>{{cite book|title=The Concept of Mind|author=Gilbert Ryle|publisher=University of Chicago Press|date=2000 |orig-date=1949|pages=156–163|isbn=978-0-226-73296-1|title-link=The Concept of Mind|author-link=Gilbert Ryle}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal|author=Gordon Gallup|title=Chimpanzees: Self recognition|journal=Science|volume=167|pages=86–87|year=1970|doi=10.1126/science.167.3914.86|pmid=4982211|issue=3914|bibcode=1970Sci...167...86G|s2cid=145295899|author-link=Gordon G. Gallup}}</ref>
paragraph: Another approach applies specifically to the study of [[self-awareness]], that is, the ability to distinguish oneself from others. In the 1970s [[Gordon G. Gallup|Gordon Gallup]] developed an operational test for self-awareness, known as the [[mirror test]]. The test examines whether animals are able to differentiate between seeing themselves in a mirror versus seeing other animals. The classic example involves placing a spot of coloring on the skin or fur near the individual's forehead and seeing if they attempt to remove it or at least touch the spot, thus indicating that they recognize that the individual they are seeing in the mirror is themselves.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Gordon Gallup|title=Chimpanzees: Self recognition|journal=Science|volume=167|pages=86–87|year=1970|doi=10.1126/science.167.3914.86|pmid=4982211|issue=3914|bibcode=1970Sci...167...86G|s2cid=145295899|author-link=Gordon G. Gallup}}</ref> Humans (older than 18 months) and other [[Hominidae|great apes]], [[bottlenose dolphin]]s, [[orca]]s, [[Columbidae|pigeons]], [[Eurasian magpie|European magpies]] and [[elephants]] have all been observed to pass this test,<ref>{{cite journal |author=David Edelman |author2=Anil Seth |year=2009 |title=Animal consciousness: a synthetic approach |journal=Trends in Neurosciences |volume=32 |issue=9 |pages=476–484 |doi=10.1016/j.tins.2009.05.008 |pmid=19716185 |s2cid=13323524}}</ref> while some other animals like [[pig]]s have been shown to find food by looking into the mirror.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Broom |first1=Donald M. |last2=Sena |first2=Hilana |last3=Moynihan |first3=Kiera L. |date= 2009|title=Pigs learn what a mirror image represents and use it to obtain information |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=78 |issue=5 |pages=1037–1041 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2009.07.027 |bibcode=2009AnBeh..78.1037B }}</ref>

paragraph: Bonwell and Eison (1991) suggested learners work collaboratively, discuss materials while [[role-playing]], [[debate]], engage in [[case study]], take part in [[cooperative learning]], or produce short written exercises, etc. The argument is "when should active learning exercises be used during instruction?". Learning|deeper learning]], understanding, and transfer.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brant |first1=George |last2=Hooper |first2=Elizabeth |last3=Sugrue |first3=Brenda |title=Which Comes First the
