ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nosek |first1=Brian A. |last2=Banaji |first2=Mahzarin R. |last3=Greenwald |first3=Anthony G. |title=Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me |journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=44–59 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44 |date=July 2002 |citeseerx=10.1.1.463.6120 |pmid=12088131 |s2cid=204321795}}</ref>
paragraph: One example of an implicit gender stereotype is that males are seen as better at mathematics than females. It has been found that men have stronger positive associations with mathematics than women, while women have stronger negative associations with mathematics and the more strongly a woman associates herself with the female gender identity, the more negative her association with mathematics.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nosek |first1=Brian A. |last2=Banaji |first2=Mahzarin R. |last3=Greenwald |first3=Anthony G. |title=Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me |journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=44–59 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44 |date=July 2002 |citeseerx=10.1.1.463.6120 |pmid=12088131 |s2cid=204321795}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="NoBaGr">{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B. A. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M. R. | last3 = Greenwald | first3 = A. G. | year = 2002 | title = Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 1| pages = 44–59 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44 | pmid = 12088131 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.463.6120 | s2cid = 204321795 }}</ref>
paragraph:
==== Gender bias in science and engineering ====
[[Implicit-association test]]s reveal an implicit association for male with science and math, and females with arts and language.<ref name="NoBaGr">{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B. A. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M. R. | last3 = Greenwald | first3 = A. G. | year = 2002 | title = Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 1| pages = 44–59 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44 | pmid = 12088131 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.463.6120 | s2cid = 204321795 }}</ref> Girls as young as nine years old have been found to hold an implicit male-math stereotype and an implicit preference for language over math.<ref name="steff">{{cite journal | last1 = Steffens | first1 = M. C. | last2 = Jelenec | first2 = P. | last3 = Noack | first3 = P. | year = 2010 | title = On the leaky math pipeline: Comparing implicit math-gender stereotypes and math withdrawal in female and male children and adolescents | journal = Journal of Educational Psychology | volume =  102| issue = 4| pages =  947–963| doi = 10.1037/a0019920 }}</ref> Women have stronger negative associations with math than men do, and the stronger females associate with a female gender identity, the more implicit negativity they have towards math.<ref name=NoBaGr /> For both men and women, the strength of these implicit stereotypes predicts both implicit and explicit math attitudes, belief in one's math ability, and SAT performance.<ref name=NoBaGr /> The strength of these implicit stereotypes in elementary-aged girls predicts academic self-concepts, academic achievement, and enrollment preferences, even more than do explicit measures.<ref name=steff /> Women with a stronger implicit gender-math stereotype were less likely to pursue a math-related career, regardless of their actual math ability or explicit gender-math stereotypes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kiefer |first1=Amy K. |last2=Sekaquaptewa |first2=Denise |title=Implicit Stereotypes, Gender Identification, and Math-Related Outcomes: A Prospective Study of Female College Students |journal=Psychological Science |date=January 2007 |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=13–18 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01841.x |pmid=17362371 |s2cid=30588471 }}</ref> This may be because women with stronger implicit gender-math stereotypes are more at risk for [[stereotype threat]]. Thus, women with strong implicit stereotypes perform much worse on a math test
section: Gender bias in science and engineering

ref_block: <ref>Goff, P. A., Jackson, M. C., Di Leone, B. A. L., Culotta, C. M., & DiTomasso, N. A. (2014). The essence of innocence: Consequences of dehumanizing Black children. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 106(4), 526–545. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0035663</ref>
paragraph: Recent research has shown that implicit biases can influence how people perceive children, particularly Black children. A study by Goff et al. (2014) found that Black boys as young as 10 years old were perceived as significantly older and less innocent than White boys of the same age. Participants in the study also judged Black children to be more responsible for their actions in hypothetical scenarios. This phenomenon, known as adultification bias, reflects an implicit stereotype that reduces empathy and increases support for punitive treatment. These biased perceptions are associated with real-world consequences, including disproportionate rates of school discipline and harsher treatment by law enforcement toward Black youth.<ref>Goff, P. A., Jackson, M. C., Di Leone, B. A. L., Culotta, C. M., & DiTomasso, N. A. (2014). The essence of innocence: Consequences of dehumanizing Black children. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 106(4), 526–545. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0035663</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B.A. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M.R. | last3 = Greenwald | first3 = A.G. | year = 2002 | title = Math=male, me=female, therefore math ≠ me | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 1| pages = 44–59 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44| pmid = 12088131 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.463.6120 | s2cid = 204321795 }}</ref>
paragraph: Despite the unexpected sex differences in evaluative gender stereotypes found in the first study, Rudman published their results which resulted in the [[Balanced Identity Theory]] which helps explain the surprising results by theorizing that [[balance-congruity]] serves to create links between existing identity associations. For instance, “Me” is associated with “good” as well as “female.” Since both “good” and “female” and linked to “Me”, they will develop a link such that “female” is associated more strongly to be “good.” In the context of Rudman’s study, women are more likely to associate strength with female since they are female and likely see themselves as strong, so the association between strength and female is stronger. In this way, perceptions and prejudices are biased towards positive conceptions of ourselves.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Greenwald | first1 = A.G. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M.R. | last3 = Rudman | first3 = L.A. | last4 = Farnham | first4 = S.D. | last5 = Nosek | first5 = B.A. | last6 = Mellott | first6 = D.S. | year = 2002 | title = A unified theory of implicit attitudes, stereotypes, self-esteem, and self-concept | url = http://psyarxiv.com/xgswc/| journal = Psychological Review | volume = 109 | issue = 1| pages = 3–25 | doi=10.1037/0033-295x.109.1.3 | pmid=11863040| citeseerx = 10.1.1.366.9580 }}</ref> However, this can also promote dissociations for imbalances in self-identity. For instance, stereotypes such as “male-math”, with their gender identity (“me-female”), can lead to dissociations (me ≠ math) as studied by Nosek, Banaji, and Greenwald (2002).<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B.A. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M.R. | last3 = Greenwald | first3 = A.G. | year = 2002 | title = Math=male, me=female, therefore math ≠ me | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 1| pages = 44–59 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44| pmid = 12088131 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.463.6120 | s2cid = 204321795 }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rentfrow PJ, Gosling SD | title = The do re mi's of everyday life: the structure and personality correlates of music preferences | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 84 | issue = 6 | pages = 1236–1256 | date = June 2003 | pmid = 12793587 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.84.6.1236 | s2cid = 16489081 }}</ref>
paragraph:
==Behavior==
Various differences in behavioral characteristics are attributed to extraverts and introverts. According to one study, extraverts tend to wear more decorative clothing, whereas introverts prefer practical, comfortable clothes.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Sharma RS |year=1980 |title=Clothing behavior, personality, and values: A correlational study |journal=Psychological Studies |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=137–42}}</ref> Extraverts are more likely to prefer more upbeat, conventional, and energetic music than introverts.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rentfrow PJ, Gosling SD | title = The do re mi's of everyday life: the structure and personality correlates of music preferences | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 84 | issue = 6 | pages = 1236–1256 | date = June 2003 | pmid = 12793587 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.84.6.1236 | s2cid = 16489081 }}</ref> Personality also influences how people arrange their work areas. In general, extraverts decorate their offices more, keep their doors open, keep extra chairs nearby, and are more likely to put dishes of candy on their desks. These are attempts to invite co-workers and encourage interaction. Introverts, in contrast, decorate less and tend to arrange their workspace to discourage social interaction.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Gosling S | date = 2008 | title = Snoop | location = New York | publisher = Basic Books | isbn = 978-0-465-02781-1 }} {{page needed|date=December 2013}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fleeson W, Malanos AB, Achille NM | title = An intraindividual process approach to the relationship between extraversion and positive affect: is acting extraverted as "good" as being extraverted? | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 6 | pages = 1409–1422 | date = December 2002 | pmid = 12500821 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.6.1409 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.317.9301 }}</ref>
paragraph:
==Implications==
Researchers have found a correlation between extraversion and self-reported happiness. That is, more extraverted people tend to report higher levels of happiness than introverts.<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Myers DG | date = July 1992 | url = https://www.psychologytoday.com/intl/articles/199207/the-secrets-happiness | title = The Secrets of Happiness | work = Psychology Today }}</ref><ref name=Pavot>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/0191-8869(90)90157-M |title=Extraversion and happiness |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_personality-and-individual-differences_1990_11_12/page/1299 |year=1990 | vauthors = Pavot W, Diener E, Fujita F |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=11 |issue=12 |pages=1299–306}}</ref> Other research has shown that being instructed to act in an extraverted manner leads to increases in positive affect, even for people who are trait-level introverts.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fleeson W, Malanos AB, Achille NM | title = An intraindividual process approach to the relationship between extraversion and positive affect: is acting extraverted as "good" as being extraverted? | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 6 | pages = 1409–1422 | date = December 2002 | pmid = 12500821 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.6.1409 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.317.9301 }}</ref>

paragraph:
=== Psychological and behavioral assessment ===
In collaboration with [[Samuel D. Gosling]], he developed the Ten-Item Personality Inventory,<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2003-12-01|title=A very brief measure of the Big-Five personality domains|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0092656603000461|journal=Journal of Research in Personality|language=en|volume=37|issue=6|pages=504–528|doi=10.1016/S0092-6566(03)00046-1|issn=0092-6566|last1=Gosling|first1=Samuel D.|last2=Rentfrow|first2=Peter J.|last3=Swann|first3=William B.|s2cid=7147133 |url-access=subscription}}</ref>  a brief measure of the [[Big Five personality traits]]. Also with Gosling, he developed the Short Test of Musical Preferences,<ref name=":2" /> an individual difference measure designed to assess musical preferences. With [[Lewis Goldberg|Lewis R. Goldberg]] and [[Daniel Levitin|Daniel J. Levitin]], he developed an audio-based measure of musical preferences in which respondents report their effective reactions to excerpts of various pieces of music.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rentfrow|first1=Peter J.|last2=Goldberg|first2=Lewis R.|last3=Stillwell|first3=David J.|last4=Kosinski|first4=Michal|last5=Gosling|first5=Samuel D.|last6=Levitin|first6=Daniel J.|date=2012-12-01|title=The Song Remains the Same: A Replication and Extension of the MUSIC Model|url=https://online.ucpress.edu/mp/article-abstract/30/2/161/62552/The-Song-Remains-the-Same-A-Replication-and?redirectedFrom=fulltext|journal=Music Perception|language=en|volume=30|issue=2|pages=161–185|doi=10.1525/mp.2012.30.2.161|issn=0730-7829|pmc=4016970|pmid=24825945}}</ref> In addition to traditional self-report measures, Rentfrow has collaborated with computer scientist [[Cecilia Mascolo]] to develop a mobile sensing platform for measuring behavior and psychological states unobtrusively.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lathia|first1=Neal|last2=Pejovic|first2=Veljko|last3=Rachuri|first3=Kiran K.|last4=Mascolo|first4=Cecilia|last5=Musolesi|first5=Mirco|last6=Rentfrow|first6=Peter J.|date=July 2013|title=Smartphones for Large-Scale Behavior Change Interventions|journal=IEEE Pervasive Computing|volume=12|issue=3|pages=66–73|doi=10.1109/MPRV.2013.56|s2cid=14134567|issn=1536-1268}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name= "Hsee & Zhang, 2004">{{cite journal |last1=Hsee |first1=Christopher K. |last2=Zhang |first2=Jiao |title=Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2004 |volume=86 |issue=5 |pages=680–695 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680|pmid=15161394 }}</ref>
paragraph: <ref name= "Hsee & Zhang, 2004">{{cite journal |last1=Hsee |first1=Christopher K. |last2=Zhang |first2=Jiao |title=Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2004 |volume=86 |issue=5 |pages=680–695 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680|pmid=15161394 }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Hsee & Zhang">{{cite journal|last1=Hsee|first1=Christopher|last2=Zhang|first2=Jiao|title=Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=2004|volume=86|issue=5|pages=680–695|ssrn=929932|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680|pmid=15161394}}</ref>
paragraph: When predictions or choices are made, explained Hsee and Zhang in their 2004 paper, people are often in the joint evaluation (JE) mode but when people experience an event, they are often in the single evaluation (SE) mode. The 'utility function' of an attribute can vary between single evaluation and joint evaluation. When people in joint evaluation make predictions or decisions for events to be experienced in single evaluation, they often resort to their joint evaluation preferences rather than single evaluation preferences and over-predict the difference that different values of an attribute will lead their happiness in single evaluation. This over-prediction is referred to as the distinction bias.<ref name="Hsee & Zhang">{{cite journal|last1=Hsee|first1=Christopher|last2=Zhang|first2=Jiao|title=Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=2004|volume=86|issue=5|pages=680–695|ssrn=929932|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680|pmid=15161394}}</ref>

<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=K.|last2=Cheng|first2=C.M.|last3=Govorun|first3=O.|last4=Stewart|first4=B.D.|date=2005|title=An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=89|issue=3|pages=277–293|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277|pmid=16248714|citeseerx=10.1.1.392.4775}}</ref>
paragraph: The act of taking the Race IAT has also been found to exacerbate the negative implicit attitudes that it seeks to assess.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hussey |first1=Ian |last2=De Houwer |first2=Jan |title=Completing a Race IAT increases implicit racial bias |date=19 November 2018 |doi=10.31234/osf.io/vxsj7 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/vxsj7/ |doi-access=free }}</ref> Results from four pre-registered experiments demonstrated that completing a Race IAT resulted in increases in White participants' negative automatic racial evaluations of Black people as measured by two different implicit measures (Single Category IAT<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Karpinksi|first1=A|last2=Steinman|first2=RB|date=2006|title=The single category implicit association test as a measure of implicit social cognition|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=91|issue=1|pages=16–32|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.91.1.16|pmid=16834477}}</ref> and the Affective Misattribution Procedure<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=K.|last2=Cheng|first2=C.M.|last3=Govorun|first3=O.|last4=Stewart|first4=B.D.|date=2005|title=An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=89|issue=3|pages=277–293|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277|pmid=16248714|citeseerx=10.1.1.392.4775}}</ref>) but did not generalize to another measure of automatic racial bias (Shooter Bias Task<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Correll|first1=J|s2cid=44055107|title=The Police Officer's Dilemma: A Decade of Research on Racial Bias in the Decision to Shoot|journal=Social and Personality Psychology Compass|date=2014|volume=8|issue=5|pages=201–213|doi=10.1111/spc3.12099}}</ref>).

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | first1 = Matthew K. | last1= Nock | first2 = Jennifer M. | last2 = Park | first3 = Christine T. | last3 = Finn | first4 = Tara L. | last4 = Deliberto | first5 = Halina J. | last5 = Dour | first6 = Mahzarin R. | last6 = Banaji | title = Measuring the Suicidal Mind: Implicit Cognition Predicts Suicidal Behavior | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 21 | issue = 4 | pages = 511–517 | doi = 10.1177/0956797610364762 | pmid = 20424092 | pmc = 5258199 | year = 2010 }}</ref>
paragraph:
====Predictive validity====
A 2009 [[meta-analysis]] lead-authored by Greenwald<ref name="IAT3"/> concluded that the IAT has [[predictive validity]] independent of the predictive validity of explicit measures. A follow-up meta-analysis lead-authored by Frederick L. Oswald criticised Greenwald's study for overestimating the correlations between IAT scores and discriminatory behavior by including studies that didn't actually measure discriminatory behavior (such as those which found a link between high IAT scores and certain brain patterns) and treating published findings in which high IAT scores correlated with better behavior toward out-group than in-group members as evidence of implicitly biased individuals overcompensating.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Oswald | first1 = F.L. | last2 = Mitchell | first2 = G. | last3 = Blanton | first3 = H. | last4 = Jaccard | first4 = J. | last5 = Tetlock | first5 = P. E. | year = 2013 | title = Predicting ethnic and racial discrimination: A meta-analysis of IAT criterion studies | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 105 | issue = 2| pages = 171–192 | doi=10.1037/a0032734| pmid = 23773046 }}</ref> Oswald's team found that implicit measures were only weakly predictive of behaviors and no better than explicit measures. Some research has found that the IAT tends to be a better predictor of behavior in socially sensitive contexts (e.g. discrimination and suicidal behaviour)<ref>{{cite journal | first1 = Matthew K. | last1= Nock | first2 = Jennifer M. | last2 = Park | first3 = Christine T. | last3 = Finn | first4 = Tara L. | last4 = Deliberto | first5 = Halina J. | last5 = Dour | first6 = Mahzarin R. | last6 = Banaji | title = Measuring the Suicidal Mind: Implicit Cognition Predicts Suicidal Behavior | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 21 | issue = 4 | pages = 511–517 | doi = 10.1177/0956797610364762 | pmid = 20424092 | pmc = 5258199 | year = 2010 }}</ref> than traditional "explicit" self-report methods,<ref name="IAT3" /> whereas explicit measures tend to be better predictors of behavior in less socially sensitive contexts (e.g. political preferences). Specifically, the IAT has been shown to predict voting behavior (e.g. ultimate candidate choice of undecided voters),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Arcuri | first1 = L. | last2 = Castelli | first2 = L. | last3 = Galdi | first3 = S. | last4 = Zogmaister | first4 = C. | last5 = Amadori | first5 = A. | s2cid = 54697838 | year = 2008 | title = Predicting the vote: Implicit attitudes as predictors of the future behavior of the decided and undecided voters | journal = Political Psychology | volume = 29 | issue = 3| pages = 369–387 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-9221.2008.00635.x| doi-access = free }}</ref> mental health (e.g. a self-injury IAT differentiated between adolescents who injured themselves and those who did not),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nock | first1 = M. K. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M. R. | s2cid = 8432841 | year = 2007a | title = Assessments of self-injurious thoughts using a behavioral test | journal = American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 164 | issue = 5| pages = 820–823 | doi=10.1176/appi.ajp.164.5.820| pmid = 17475742 }}</ref> medical outcomes (e.g. medical recommendations by physicians),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Green | first1 = A. R. | last2 = Carney | first2 = D. R. | last3 = Pallin | first3 = D. J. | last4 = Ngo | first4 = L. H. | last5 = Raymond | first5 = K. L. | last6 = Iezzoni | first6 = L. ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=B. Keith|last2=Cheng|first2=Clara Michelle|last3=Govorun|first3=Olesya|last4=Stewart|first4=Brandon D.|s2cid=12209281|date=September 2005|title=An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=89|issue=3|pages=277–293|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277|pmid=16248714|issn=1939-1315|citeseerx=10.1.1.392.4775}}</ref>
paragraph: Affective priming, also called '''affect priming''', is a type of response [[Priming (psychology)|priming]] and was first proposed by [[Russell H. Fazio]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Fazio|first=Russell H.|date=March 2001|title=On the automatic activation of associated evaluations: An overview|journal=Cognition & Emotion|volume=15|issue=2|pages=115–141|doi=10.1080/02699930125908|s2cid=7087714 |issn=0269-9931}}</ref> This type of priming entails the evaluation of people, ideas, objects, goods, etc., not only based on the physical features of those things, but also on affective context. The affective context may come from previous life experiences, and therefore, primes may arouse emotions rather than ideas. Most research and concepts about affective priming derive from the affective priming paradigm, which looks to make judgments of neutral affective targets following positive, neutral, or negative primes.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Gibbons|first1=Henning|last2=Seib-Pfeifer|first2=Laura-Effi|last3=Koppehele-Gossel|first3=Judith|last4=Schnuerch|first4=Robert|date=2017-09-21|title=Affective priming and cognitive load: Event-related potentials suggest an interplay of implicit affect misattribution and strategic inhibition|journal=Psychophysiology|volume=55|issue=4|article-number=e13009|doi=10.1111/psyp.13009|pmid=28940207|issn=0048-5772}}</ref> A prominent derivation of affective priming paradigm is the [[Affect misattribution procedure|Affect Misattribution Procedure]] (AMP), developed by Payne, Cheng, Govorun, and Stewart.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=B. Keith|last2=Cheng|first2=Clara Michelle|last3=Govorun|first3=Olesya|last4=Stewart|first4=Brandon D.|s2cid=12209281|date=September 2005|title=An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=89|issue=3|pages=277–293|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277|pmid=16248714|issn=1939-1315|citeseerx=10.1.1.392.4775}}</ref> The main idea of AMP is to measure [[implicit attitude]]s, therefore, if the evaluation of the prime stimuli of an object is positive, it is said that the person has a positive attitude toward the object exposed.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Understanding priming effects in social psychology|editor=Molden, Daniel C.|isbn=978-1-4625-1938-5|oclc=891081586}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="ref17">{{cite journal | last1 = Payne | first1 = B.K. | last2 = Cheng | first2 = C.M. | last3 = Govorun | first3 = O. | last4 = Stewart | first4 = B.D. | year = 2005 | title = An inkblot for attitudes: Affective misattribution as implicit measurement | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 89 | issue = 3| pages = 277–293 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277 | pmid=16248714| citeseerx = 10.1.1.392.4775 }}</ref>
paragraph: The Affect Misattribution Procedure relies on participant ratings of neutral stimuli as an indirect measure of implicit attitudes rather than latency or accuracy measures. In the procedure, participants are first presented with a stimulus (usually an image or word), for either a brief visible period or subliminally, which is suspected to elicit a positive or negative attitude. Directly afterwards, participants are presented with a neutral stimulus (most often a [[Chinese character|Chinese pictograph]]) which they are asked to rate as either more or less, in this case visually, pleasing than an average stimulus. During these trials, the positive or negative affect in response to the priming image is misattributed or '[[Psychological projection|projected]]' onto the neutral stimulus such that it is rated as more or less pleasing than would be expected from solitary presentation.<ref name="ref17">{{cite journal | last1 = Payne | first1 = B.K. | last2 = Cheng | first2 = C.M. | last3 = Govorun | first3 = O. | last4 = Stewart | first4 = B.D. | year = 2005 | title = An inkblot for attitudes: Affective misattribution as implicit measurement | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 89 | issue = 3| pages = 277–293 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277 | pmid=16248714| citeseerx = 10.1.1.392.4775 }}</ref> Neutral stimuli which are rated as more visually pleasing indicate that the preceding concept presented in the prime stimuli are associated with positive valence. A full demonstration of the AMP procedure can be found in the external links below.

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Payne |first1=B. Keith |last2=Burkley |first2=Melissa A. |last3=Stokes |first3=Mark B. |date=2008 |title=Why do implicit and explicit attitude tests diverge? The role of structural fit. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.16 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=16–31 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.16 |pmid=18179315 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph:
===== Structural Fit =====
Implicit-explicit attitude discrepancies have also been reported as a result of improper testing when measuring the two types of attitudes of the same object. The structural-fit hypothesis posits that due to the different structures and formats of direct and indirect measures, implicit and explicit attitudes will not be significantly correlated. However, when the measures include similar formats and features, that is when they will be more congruent. Researchers have argued that indirect measures (such as the IAT) and direct measures (self-reports) result in contrasting implicit and explicit attitudes because they ask about attitudes in different ways. In accordance with the structural-fit hypothesis, when the two types of measures ask about attitudes in similar ways, the association between the two attitudes will be more consistent. Therefore, suggestions such as changing measures so that it focuses on attitudes towards certain aspects of an object, rather than the general object itself, may be beneficial when investigating the difference. Much of this explanation yearns for more research, as it is minimal. However, there has been supporting evidence that when measures are adjusted to match structure, implicit and explicit attitudes are more correlated.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Payne |first1=B. Keith |last2=Burkley |first2=Melissa A. |last3=Stokes |first3=Mark B. |date=2008 |title=Why do implicit and explicit attitude tests diverge? The role of structural fit. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.16 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=16–31 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.16 |pmid=18179315 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olson |first1=Michael A. |last2=Fazio |first2=Russell H. |author-link2=Russell H. Fazio |title=Implicit Attitude Formation Through Classical Conditioning |journal=Psychological Science |date=September 2001 |volume=12 |issue=5 |pages=413–417 |doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00376|pmid=11554676 }}</ref>
paragraph:
=== Experiences and socialization ===
Earlier research findings on implicit attitudes show that [[socialization]]<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Stereotype and Prejudice: Their automatic and controlled components|last = Devine|first = P. G|date = 1989|journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.56.1.5 |volume=56 |pages=5–18| s2cid=33975027 }}</ref> and reflections of past experiences<ref name=ref1 /> may be responsible for the development or manifestation of longer lasting implicit attitudes. As an example, a 2004 study found that individuals who were primarily raised by their mothers showed a more positive implicit attitude towards women rather than men.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rudman |first1=Laurie A. |last2=Goodwin |first2=Stephanie A.  |author-link1=Laurie A. Rudman|title=Gender Differences in Automatic In-Group Bias: Why Do Women Like Women More Than Men Like Men? |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2004 |volume=87 |issue=4 |pages=494–509 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.87.4.494|pmid=15491274 }}</ref> Furthermore, Olson and [[Russell H. Fazio|Fazio]] et al. in 2001 and 2002 suggested that these implicit attitudes are a result of repeated pairings of positive or negative stimuli with an object; more pairings of positive stimuli would result in a more positive implicit attitude and vice versa.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olson |first1=Michael A. |last2=Fazio |first2=Russell H. |author-link2=Russell H. Fazio |title=Implicit Attitude Formation Through Classical Conditioning |journal=Psychological Science |date=September 2001 |volume=12 |issue=5 |pages=413–417 |doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00376|pmid=11554676 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fazio |first1=Russell H. |last2=Olson |first2=Michael A. |title=Implicit Measures in Social Cognition Research: Their Meaning and Use |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |date=June 10, 2002 |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=297–327 |doi=10.1146/annurev.psych.54.101601.145225|pmid=12172003 }}</ref> This finding supports the fundamental principles of [[classical conditioning]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title = Implicit attitudes 101: Theoretical and empirical Insights|last = Devos|first = T.|publisher = Psychology Press|year = 2008|location = New York|chapter = Attitudes and attitude change |pages=61–84}}</ref>

<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal| vauthors = Loftus EF, Pickrell JE |date=1995-12-01|title=The Formation of False Memories|url=https://www.healio.com/psychiatry/journals/psycann/1995-12-25-12/%7B2060e727-5296-43bd-8580-55b09b96f074%7D/the-formation-of-false-memories|journal=Psychiatric Annals|language=en|volume=25|issue=12|pages=720–725|doi=10.3928/0048-5713-19951201-07|s2cid=59286093 |issn=0048-5713|url-access=subscription}}</ref>
paragraph: False memories are memories that individuals believe and recall as true that, in fact, never occurred. Often, people form false memories for details of events after hearing others mistakenly report information about an event. For example, participants who watch a video of a crime featuring a blue car but hear the car misleadingly referred to as white after the fact may create a false memory of a white car present at the scene of the crime, rather than a blue one.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal| vauthors = Loftus EF, Pickrell JE |date=1995-12-01|title=The Formation of False Memories|url=https://www.healio.com/psychiatry/journals/psycann/1995-12-25-12/%7B2060e727-5296-43bd-8580-55b09b96f074%7D/the-formation-of-false-memories|journal=Psychiatric Annals|language=en|volume=25|issue=12|pages=720–725|doi=10.3928/0048-5713-19951201-07|s2cid=59286093 |issn=0048-5713|url-access=subscription}}</ref> False memories can range from small details about an event to entire events that never happened, such as being lost in a crowded shopping mall as a child.<ref name=":1" />

ref_block: <ref name="Pronin 2006">{{Cite journal|last1=Pronin|first1=Emily|last2=Ross|first2=Lee|date=2006|title=Temporal differences in trait self-ascription: When the self is seen as an other.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=90|issue=2|pages=197–209|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.90.2.197|pmid=16536646|issn=1939-1315}}</ref>
paragraph:
{{Short description|Research that examines the processes of thinking of oneself in the future}}
In the [[psychology of self]], the '''future self''' concerns the processes and consequences associated with thinking about [[Self|oneself]] in the [[future]]. People think about their future selves similarly to how they think about other people.<ref name="Pronin 2006">{{Cite journal|last1=Pronin|first1=Emily|last2=Ross|first2=Lee|date=2006|title=Temporal differences in trait self-ascription: When the self is seen as an other.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=90|issue=2|pages=197–209|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.90.2.197|pmid=16536646|issn=1939-1315}}</ref><ref name="Pronin 2007" /><ref name="Burum 2016">{{Cite journal|last1=Burum|first1=Bethany A.|last2=Gilbert|first2=Daniel T.|last3=Wilson|first3=Timothy D.|date=2016|title=Becoming stranger: When future selves join the out-group.|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General|language=en|volume=145|issue=9|pages=1132–1140|doi=10.1037/xge0000193|pmid=27559617|issn=1939-2222|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Hershfield|first=Hal E|date=2019|title=The self over time|journal=Current Opinion in Psychology|volume=26|pages=72–75|doi=10.1016/j.copsyc.2018.06.004|pmid=29958146|s2cid=49612413 |issn=2352-250X}}</ref> The extent to which people feel psychologically connected (e.g., [[Similarity (psychology)|similarity]], closeness) to their future self influences how well they treat their future self.<ref name="Urminsky 2017">{{Cite journal|last=Urminsky|first=Oleg|date=2017|title=The Role of Psychological Connectedness to the Future Self in Decisions Over Time|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|language=en|volume=26|issue=1|pages=34–39|doi=10.1177/0963721416668810|s2cid=151894499|issn=0963-7214|doi-access=free}}</ref> When people feel connected to their future self, they are more likely to save for [[retirement]], make healthy decisions, and avoid ethical transgressions.<ref name="Oettingen 2018">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=srk8DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA89|title=The Psychology of Thinking about the Future|last1=Oettingen|first1=Gabriele|last2=Sevincer|first2=A. Timur|last3=Gollwitzer|first3=Peter M.|date=2018-03-08|publisher=Guilford Publications|isbn=9781462534418|language=en}}</ref> Interventions that increase feelings of connectedness with future selves can improve future-oriented decision making across these domains.<ref name="Hershfield 2018">{{Cite journal|last1=Hershfield|first1=Hal E.|last2=John|first2=Elicia M.|last3=Reiff|first3=Joseph S.|date=2018-09-06|title=

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last=Norton |first=Michael I. |author-link=Michael Norton (professor) |last2=Frost |first2=Jeana H. |last3=Ariely |first3=Dan |author-link3=Dan Ariely |date=2007 |title=Less is more: The lure of ambiguity, or why familiarity breeds contempt. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.92.1.97 |journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] |language=en |volume=92 |issue=1 |pages=97–105 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.92.1.97 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph:
[[File:Euler diagram used to represent the propinquity effect.svg|225px|center]]
The sets are basically any relevant subject matter about a person, persons, or non-persons, depending on the context. Propinquity can be more than just physical distance. Residents of an apartment building living near one of the building's stairways, for example, tend to have more friends from other floors than those living further from the stairway.<ref name="auto"/> The propinquity effect is usually explained by the [[mere exposure effect]], which holds that the more exposure a stimulus gets, the more likeable it becomes. There is a requirement for the mere exposure effect to influence the propinquity effect, and that is that the exposure is positive. If the resident has repeatedly negative experiences with a person then the propinquity effect has a far less chance of happening (Norton, Frost, & Ariely, 2007).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Norton |first=Michael I. |author-link=Michael Norton (professor) |last2=Frost |first2=Jeana H. |last3=Ariely |first3=Dan |author-link3=Dan Ariely |date=2007 |title=Less is more: The lure of ambiguity, or why familiarity breeds contempt. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.92.1.97 |journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] |language=en |volume=92 |issue=1 |pages=97–105 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.92.1.97 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Harmon-Jones |first1=Eddie |last2=Harmon-Jones |first2=Cindy |last3=Fearn |first3=Meghan |last4=Sigelman |first4=Jonathan D. |last5=Johnson |first5=Peter |date=2008 |title=Left frontal cortical activation and spreading of alternatives: Tests of the action-based model of dissonance. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |pmid=18179314 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph:
== Role in attitude change theories ==
Attitude objects are central to attitude change theories as well. For instance, the action-based model of dissonance<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Harmon-Jones |first1=Eddie |last2=Harmon-Jones |first2=Cindy |last3=Fearn |first3=Meghan |last4=Sigelman |first4=Jonathan D. |last5=Johnson |first5=Peter |date=2008 |title=Left frontal cortical activation and spreading of alternatives: Tests of the action-based model of dissonance. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |pmid=18179314 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref> describes how conflicting beliefs about an attitude object can create a state of dissonance, which leads to efforts to align one's attitudes and reduce discomfort.This theory highlights how attitude objects, particularly those tied to strong beliefs or values, can drive efforts to resolve inconsistencies and achieve a sense of internal harmony.

paragraph: Research has suggested multiple routes that cognitive dissonance can be reduced. One widely studied strategy is self-affirmation, which involves focusing on positive aspects of the self to protect one’s self-integrity when faced with threatening information.<ref name="Steele 1988" /> While effective in some contexts, studies suggest that when multiple dissonance-reduction strategies are available, individuals often prefer to directly alter their attitudes or behaviours rather than engage in self-affirmation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Harmon-Jones |first1=Eddie |last2=Harmon-Jones |first2=Cindy |last3=Fearn |first3=Meghan |last4=Sigelman |first4=Jonathan D. |last5=Johnson |first5=Peter |date=2008 |title=Left frontal cortical activation and spreading of alternatives: Tests of the action-based model of dissonance. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.1 |pmid=18179314 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Stone 1997">{{cite journal |last1=Stone |first1=J |last2=Wiegand |first2=AW |last3=Cooper |first3=J |last4=Aronson |first4=E |year=1997 |title=When exemplification fails: hypocrisy and the motive for self-integrity |journal=J. Pers. Soc. Psychol. |volume=72 |issue=1 |pages=54–65 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.72.1.54 |pmid=9008374}}</ref> Interestingly, individuals with high self-esteem, who theoretically have more internal resources for self-affirmation, also tend to favour modifying their cognitions over relying on affirming self-worth.<ref name="Gibbons 1997">{{cite journal |last1=Gibbons |first1=FX |last2=Eggleston |first2=TJ |last3=Benthin |first3=AC |year=1997 |title=Cognitive reactions to smoking relapse: the reciprocal relation between dissonance and self-esteem |journal=J. Pers. Soc. Psychol. |volume=72 |issue=1 |pages=184–95 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.72.1.184 |pmid=9008380}}</ref>

W. |last2=Finkel |first2=E. J. }}</ref>
paragraph: Research by Paul Eastwick and Eli Finkel (relationship psychologist)<ref name="veconomist">{{cite news|title=Unorthodox advice for rescuing a marriage|url=https://www.economist.com/news/books-and-arts/21730135-eli-finkel-counsels-lowering-expectations-hard-times-esther-perel-advises-grappling|newspaper=[[The Economist]]|date=12 October 2017}}</ref> at [[Northwestern University]] also undermined the idea that subjects have direct introspective awareness of what attracts them to other people. These researchers examined male and female subjects' reports of what they found attractive. Men typically reported that physical attractiveness was crucial while women identified earning potential as most important. These subjective reports did not predict their actual choices in a [[speed dating]] context, or their dating behaviour in a one-month follow-up.<ref>{{cite journal |date=February 2008 |title=Sex differences in mate preferences revisited: Do people know what they initially desire in a romantic partner? |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=94 |issue=2 |pages=245–264 |doi= 10.1037/0022-3514.94.2.245 |pmid=18211175 |last1=Eastwick |first1=P. W. |last2=Finkel |first2=E. J. }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="ubc-emotionlab.ca">{{cite journal|last1=Tracy|first1=Jessica L.|last2=Robins|first2=Richard W.|title=The nonverbal expression of pride: Evidence for cross-cultural recognition |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2008 |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=516–530 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.3.516 |pmid=18284295|url=http://ubc-emotionlab.ca/wp-content/uploads/2011/11/tracy-robins-jpsp-2008-pride-culture.pdf}}</ref>
paragraph: One of the strongest pieces of evidence that supports this model was a study conducted by [[Paul Ekman]], where members of a preliterate tribe in [[Papua New Guinea]] reliably recognized the facial expressions of individuals from the United States. Culturally isolated and with no exposure to US media, there was no possibility of cross-cultural transmission to the Papuan tribesmen.<ref name="ubc-emotionlab.ca">{{cite journal|last1=Tracy|first1=Jessica L.|last2=Robins|first2=Richard W.|title=The nonverbal expression of pride: Evidence for cross-cultural recognition |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2008 |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=516–530 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.3.516 |pmid=18284295|url=http://ubc-emotionlab.ca/wp-content/uploads/2011/11/tracy-robins-jpsp-2008-pride-culture.pdf}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Van Dijk | first1 = E. | last2 = Van Kleef | first2 = G. A. | last3 = Steinel | first3 = W. | last4 = Van Beest | first4 = I. | year = 2008 | title = A social functional approach to emotions in bargaining: when communicating anger pays and when it backfires | url = https://dare.uva.nl/personal/pure/en/publications/a-social-functional-approach-to-emotions-in-bargaining-when-communicating-anger-pays-and-when-it-backfires(cb9af110-8341-4b5e-94dd-2f464eee3aa6).html| journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 94 | issue = 4| pages = 600–14 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.94.4.600 | pmid = 18361674 | s2cid = 13718600 }}</ref>
paragraph: Given this communicative role of emotions, emotions facilitate learning by serving as incentives or deterrents for certain kinds of actions or behaviors. For example, when children see how their parents or friends emotionally respond to things they do, they learn what types of actions and behaviors are likely to lead to desirable outcomes, including positive emotional responses from those around them. This communicative role is important in informing how people behave in both professional and intimate adult human relationships as well, since emotions can convey how a particular relationship or interaction is evolving in positive or negative directions. For example, anger can signal that an individual or group has reached its limit within a negotiation, and can immediately structure the behavioral responses from the opposite party.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Van Dijk | first1 = E. | last2 = Van Kleef | first2 = G. A. | last3 = Steinel | first3 = W. | last4 = Van Beest | first4 = I. | year = 2008 | title = A social functional approach to emotions in bargaining: when communicating anger pays and when it backfires | url = https://dare.uva.nl/personal/pure/en/publications/a-social-functional-approach-to-emotions-in-bargaining-when-communicating-anger-pays-and-when-it-backfires(cb9af110-8341-4b5e-94dd-2f464eee3aa6).html| journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 94 | issue = 4| pages = 600–14 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.94.4.600 | pmid = 18361674 | s2cid = 13718600 }}</ref> Meanwhile, sadness can communicate the readiness to disengage from a goal,<ref name=":9" /> and the potential for social withdrawal from a person or group,<ref name=":10">{{cite journal | last1 = Van Dijk | first1 = W. W. | last2 = Zeelenberg | first2 = M. | year = 2002 | title = Investigating the appraisal patterns of regret and disappointment | url = https://research.vu.nl/en/publications/6d0324bf-70d0-4095-9e6a-b1052b904324| journal = Motivation and Emotion | volume = 26 | issue = 4| pages = 321–331 | doi = 10.1023/a:1022823221146 | s2cid = 55491643 }}</ref> thereby conveying that a potentially valuable relationship is at risk.

A. | last3 = Steinel | first3 = W. | last4 = Van Beest | first4 = I. | year = 2008 | title = A social functional approach to emotions in bargaining: when communicating anger pays and when it backfires | url = https://dare.uva.nl/personal/pure/en/publications/a-social-functional-approach-to-emotions-in-bargaining-when-communicating-anger-pays-and-when-it-backfires(cb9af110-8341-4b5e-94dd-2f464eee3aa6).html| journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 94 | issue = 4| pages = 600–14 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.94.4.600 | pmid = 18361674 | s2cid = 13718600 }}</ref> Meanwhile, sadness can communicate the readiness to disengage from a goal,<ref name=":9" /> and the potential for social withdrawal from a person or group,<ref name=":10">{{cite journal | last1 = Van Dijk | first1 = W. W. | last2 = Zeelenberg | first2 = M. | year = 2002 | title = Investigating the appraisal patterns of regret and disappointment | url = https://research.vu.nl/en/publications/6d0324bf-70d0-4095-9e6a-b1052b904324| journal = Motivation and Emotion | volume = 26 | issue = 4| pages = 321–331 | doi = 10.1023/a:1022823221146 | s2cid = 55491643 }}</ref> thereby conveying that a potentially valuable relationship is at risk.

ref_block: <ref name=":0">Purdie-Vaughns, Valerie; Steele, Claude M.; Davies, Paul G.; Ditlmann, Ruth; Crosby, Jennifer Randall (2008). "Social identity contingencies: How diversity cues signal threat or safety for African Americans in mainstream institutions". ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology''. '''94''' (4): 615–630. {{doi|10.1037/0022-3514.94.4.615}}. {{issn|1939-1315}}.</ref>
paragraph: '''Identity safety cues''' are aspects of an environment or setting that signal to members of stigmatized groups that the threat of discrimination is limited within that environment and / or that their social identities are welcomed and valued.<ref name=":0">Purdie-Vaughns, Valerie; Steele, Claude M.; Davies, Paul G.; Ditlmann, Ruth; Crosby, Jennifer Randall (2008). "Social identity contingencies: How diversity cues signal threat or safety for African Americans in mainstream institutions". ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology''. '''94''' (4): 615–630. {{doi|10.1037/0022-3514.94.4.615}}. {{issn|1939-1315}}.</ref> Identity safety cues have been shown to reduce the negative impacts of [[Social identity threat|social identity threats]], which are when people experience situations where they feel devalued on the basis of a social identity (see [[stereotype threat]]).<ref name=":6">Steele, C. M., Spencer, S. J., & Aronson, J. (2002). "Contending with group image: The psychology of stereotype and social identity threat". In ''Advances in Experimental Social Psychology'' (Vol. 34, pp. 379-440). Academic Press.</ref> Such threats have been shown to undermine performance in academic and work-related contexts and make members of stigmatized groups feel as though they do not belong.<ref>Steele, C. M., & Aronson, J. (1995). "Stereotype threat and the intellectual test performance of African Americans". ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', ''69''(5), 797.</ref> Identity safety cues have been proposed as a way of alleviating the negative impact of stereotype threat or other social identity threats, reducing disparities in academic performance for members of stigmatized groups (see [[achievement gaps in the United States]]), and reducing [[health disparities]] caused by identity related stressors.

ref_block:
<ref>{{Cite journal
 | author = Kay, A.C. |author2=Gaucher, D. |author3=Napier, J.L. |author4=Callan, M.J. |author5=Laurin, K.
 | title = God and the government: Testing a compensatory control mechanism for the support of external systems
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | year = 2008
 | volume = 95
 | issue = 1
 | pages = 18–35
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.18
| pmid = 18605849
 }}</ref>
paragraph:
In the experimental setting, researchers have also tested compensatory control in regard to individuals' perceptions of external systems, such as religion or government. For example, Kay and colleagues<ref>{{Cite journal
 | author = Kay, A.C. |author2=Gaucher, D. |author3=Napier, J.L. |author4=Callan, M.J. |author5=Laurin, K.
 | title = God and the government: Testing a compensatory control mechanism for the support of external systems
 | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]
 | year = 2008
 | volume = 95
 | issue = 1
 | pages = 18–35
 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.18
| pmid = 18605849
 }}</ref> found that in a laboratory setting, individuals are more likely to endorse broad external systems (e.g., religion or sociopolitical systems) that impose order and control on their lives when they are induced with lowered levels of personal control. In this study, researchers suggest that when a person's personal control is lessened, their motivation to believe in order is threatened, resulting in compensation of this threat through adherence to other external sources of control.

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kay|first1=Aaron C.|last2=Gaucher|first2=Danielle|last3=Napier|first3=Jamie L.|last4=Callan|first4=Mitchell J.|last5=Laurin|first5=Kristin|date=2008|title=God and the government: Testing a compensatory control mechanism for the support of external systems.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=95|issue=1|pages=18–35|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.18|pmid=18605849|issn=1939-1315}}</ref>
paragraph:
=== Product acquisition ===
People more strongly linked to situations where they are able to feel stronger connected and influenced. These feelings are compounded when people are suffering from control deprivation. In line with the [[compensatory control theory]], individuals will resort to a wide range of strategies to regain control. These strategies do not have to be linked to the source of the control deprivation.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Landau|first1=Mark J.|last2=Kay|first2=Aaron C.|last3=Whitson|first3=Jennifer A.|date=May 2015|title=Compensatory control and the appeal of a structured world.|journal=Psychological Bulletin|volume=141|issue=3|pages=694–722|doi=10.1037/a0038703|pmid=25688696|issn=1939-1455|doi-access=free}}</ref> Individuals will aim to complete different or unrelated tasks that the individual is able to gain control over. A study where subjects were asked to complete simple meaningless tasks of deciphering a stimuli within visual noise allowed the subjects to regain control through completing simple tasks that give a reliable and desired outcome that reaffirms a level of control that was previously missing.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kay|first1=Aaron C.|last2=Gaucher|first2=Danielle|last3=Napier|first3=Jamie L.|last4=Callan|first4=Mitchell J.|last5=Laurin|first5=Kristin|date=2008|title=God and the government: Testing a compensatory control mechanism for the support of external systems.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=95|issue=1|pages=18–35|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.18|pmid=18605849|issn=1939-1315}}</ref><ref name=":5" /> This is hypothesised to be effective in restoring mental strength as it provides evidence to the individual that they are able to influence the world around them and that they do have an intrinsic level of control and belonging.<ref name=":5" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Langer|first=Ellen J.|s2cid=30043741|date=1975|title=The illusion of control.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=32|issue=2|pages=311–328|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.32.2.311|issn=0022-3514}}</ref> As a direct result individuals are more likely to purchase and consume products that link to their intrinsic need for control.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Chen|first1=Charlene Y.|last2=Lee|first2=Leonard|last3=Yap|first3=Andy J.|date=2016-11-09|title=Control Deprivation Motivates Acquisition of Utilitarian Products|journal=Journal of Consumer Research|language=en|article-number=ucw068|doi=10.1093/jcr/ucw068|issn=0093-5301}}</ref> The study, conducted by Charlene Y Chen, Leonard Lee and Andy J Yap focuses on how control deprivation can be used to entice the subjects to purchase products. Through the running of multiple experiments, the study found that individuals after being submitted to a control deprived situation are more likely to purchase a utilitarian products over a hedonic product, this is hypothesised to be a method to be able to regain the control that had been lost through being deprived,<ref name=":6" /> with the utilitarian product being picked to represent more control that is missing as well as the long term use and effectiveness of these products<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Khan|first=Uzma|date=August 2004|title=A Behavioral Decision Theoretic Perspective on Hedonic and Utilitarian Choice|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/255663016|journal=Inside Consumption: Frontiers of Research on Consumer Motives, Goals, and Desires (Ed.)|pages=1–36|via=Research Gate}}</ref> However this study

ref_block: <ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Monin |first1=Benoît |last2=Sawyer |first2=Pamela J. |last3=Marquez |first3=Matthew J. |date=July 2008 |title=The rejection of moral rebels: Resenting those who do the right thing. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=95 |issue=1 |pages=76–93 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |pmid=18605853 |issn=1939-1315}}</ref>
paragraph: The groundwork for do-gooder derogation was laid by Monin, Sawyer, and Marquez (2008), in a study on what they termed the "rejection of moral rebels". Participants who had complied with a morally questionable task – identifying a guilty suspect in a racially biased criminal lineup – subsequently rated a confederate who had refused to comply as more hostile and self-righteous than those who had not faced the same moral conflict<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Monin |first1=Benoît |last2=Sawyer |first2=Pamela J. |last3=Marquez |first3=Matthew J. |date=July 2008 |title=The rejection of moral rebels: Resenting those who do the right thing. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=95 |issue=1 |pages=76–93 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |pmid=18605853 |issn=1939-1315}}</ref>. This demonstrated that witnessing someone do the right thing can provoke resentment rather than admiration among those who chose not to act similarly, and is considered the empirical precursor to do-gooder derogation.

ref_block: <ref name="MoninSawyerMarquez">{{Cite journal |last1=Monin |first1=Benoît |last2=Sawyer |first2=Pamela J. |last3=Marquez |first3=Matthew J. |date=2008 |title=The rejection of moral rebels: Resenting those who do the right thing. |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2008-08084-006 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=95 |pages=76–93 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |pmid=18605853 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>
paragraph: Other research looked at morally uncomfortable decision-making scenarios, for example a criminal lineup where the obvious guilty suspect is the only African American suspect and participants were asked to pick out the guilty suspect. Most participants followed the instructions, but 'moral do-gooders' refused to, on the basis that the activity was 'offensive'. The moral do-gooders were disliked by the rest of the group and rated more negatively in the first experiment. However, later in the study, participants were assured of their moral standing and the validity of their own decisions despite that of the moral do-gooder, and they were less sensitive to moral reproach, rating the do-gooders less negatively.<ref name="MoninSawyerMarquez">{{Cite journal |last1=Monin |first1=Benoît |last2=Sawyer |first2=Pamela J. |last3=Marquez |first3=Matthew J. |date=2008 |title=The rejection of moral rebels: Resenting those who do the right thing. |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2008-08084-006 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=95 |pages=76–93 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.76 |pmid=18605853 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

paragraph:
{{Short description|Asymmetric similarity measure on sets}}
The '''Tversky index''', named after [[Amos Tversky]],<ref>{{cite journal |last=Tversky |first=Amos |title=Features of Similarity |journal=Psychological Review |volume=84 |number=4 |year=1977 |pages=327–352 |url=http://www.cogsci.ucsd.edu/~coulson/203/tversky-features.pdf |doi=10.1037/0033-295x.84.4.327}}</ref> is an asymmetric [[similarity measure]] on [[set theory|sets]] that compares a variant to a prototype. The Tversky index can be seen as a generalization of the [[Sørensen–Dice coefficient]] and the [[Jaccard index]].

ref_block: <ref name="Kahneman 1986">{{Cite journal | last1 = Kahneman | first1 = Daniel | last2 = Miller | first2 = Dale T. | title = Norm theory: comparing reality to its alternatives | journal = [[Psychological Review]] | volume = 93 | issue = 2 | pages = 136–153 | doi = 10.1037/0033-295x.93.2.136 | date = April 1986 }}</ref>
paragraph:
=== Counterfactual thinking ===
[[Counterfactual thinking]] occurs when an individual mentally morphs, restructures, or changes events. It is possible that unacknowledged rape victims use counterfactual thinking to cognitively reorganize the experience into something other than rape. Victims that exhibit counterfactual thinking typically reorganize their thought patterns by thinking of ways the situation could have been worse. Others think about ways they could have prevented the situation from occurring instead of thinking about the experience itself.<ref name="Kahneman 1986">{{Cite journal | last1 = Kahneman | first1 = Daniel | last2 = Miller | first2 = Dale T. | title = Norm theory: comparing reality to its alternatives | journal = [[Psychological Review]] | volume = 93 | issue = 2 | pages = 136–153 | doi = 10.1037/0033-295x.93.2.136 | date = April 1986 }}</ref>

ref_block: <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kahneman |first1=D. |last2=Miller |first2=D.T. |year=1986 |title=Norm theory: Comparing reality to its alternatives. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=93 |issue=2 |pages=136–153 |doi=10.1037/0033-295x.93.2.136}}</ref>
paragraph:
==Mechanism==
'''Norm theory and reference point.''' [[Daniel Kahneman|Kahneman]] and Miller pointed that people tended to regard omissions as a norm, a neutral reference point, and actions as an abnormal activity<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kahneman |first1=D. |last2=Miller |first2=D.T. |year=1986 |title=Norm theory: Comparing reality to its alternatives. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=93 |issue=2 |pages=136–153 |doi=10.1037/0033-295x.93.2.136}}</ref>. This distinction stemmed from people's easier imagination of behaviors they didn't adapt (or the omission situation) than what they carried out. For the abnormal, they demonstrated more sensitive feelings, targeting the benefits that should be there and thus resulting in the amplification of the loss and foregone benefits from commissions.<ref name=":3" />

ref_block: <ref name="Birch-Bloom">{{cite journal |last1=Birch |first1=S. A.J. |last2=Bloom |first2=P. |date=2007 |title=The Curse of Knowledge in Reasoning About False Beliefs |url= |journal=Psychological Science |volume=18 |issue=5 |pages=382–386 |citeseerx=10.1.1.583.5677 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01909.x |pmid=17576275 |s2cid=18588234 }}</ref>
paragraph: Susan Birch and [[Paul Bloom (psychologist)|Paul Bloom]] similarly found a curse of knowledge in a study involving [[Yale University]] students. Participants were told one sister (Denise) moved her sister's (Vicki's) violin without Vicki knowing. When participants are told where Denise placed the violin, they are more likely to think Vicki will first look for the violin in that new location. The curse of knowledge was less pronounced when Denise put the violin in a less plausible place.<ref name="Birch-Bloom">{{cite journal |last1=Birch |first1=S. A.J. |last2=Bloom |first2=P. |date=2007 |title=The Curse of Knowledge in Reasoning About False Beliefs |url= |journal=Psychological Science |volume=18 |issue=5 |pages=382–386 |citeseerx=10.1.1.583.5677 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01909.x |pmid=17576275 |s2cid=18588234 }}</ref> However, a replication study conducted in 2014 found that this finding was not reliably reproducible across seven experiments with large sample sizes, and the true effect size of this phenomenon was less than half of that reported in the original findings. Therefore, it is suggested that "the influence of plausibility on the curse of knowledge in adults appears to be small enough that its impact on real-life perspective-taking may need to be reevaluated."<ref>{{cite journal|title=Do Adults Show a Curse of Knowledge in False-Belief Reasoning? A Robust Estimate of the True Effect Size|first1=Rachel A.|last1=Ryskin|first2=Sarah|last2=Brown-Schmidt|date=25 March 2014|journal=PLOS ONE|volume=9|issue=3|article-number=e92406|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0092406|pmid = 24667826|pmc=3965426|bibcode=2014PLoSO...992406R|doi-access=free}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Fischhoff">{{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance|date=1975|volume=1|issue=3|pages=288–299|doi=10.1037/0096-1523.1.3.288 }} Reprinted: {{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|pmid=12897366|pmc=1743746|volume=12|issue=4|year=2003|journal=Qual Saf Health Care|pages=304–11|doi=10.1136/qhc.12.4.304}}</ref>
paragraph: Such research drew from [[Baruch Fischhoff]]'s work in 1975 surrounding [[hindsight bias]], a cognitive bias that knowing the outcome of a certain event makes it seem more predictable.<ref name="Camerer-Loewenstein-Weber">{{cite journal|last1=Camerer|first1=Colin|last2=Loewenstein|first2=George|last3=Weber|first3=Martin|title=The Curse of Knowledge in Economic Settings: An Experimental Analysis|journal=Journal of Political Economy|date=1989|volume=97 |issue=5|pages=1232–1254|doi=10.1086/261651|url=http://authors.library.caltech.edu/22171/1/1831894%5B1%5D.pdf|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150306005841/http://authors.library.caltech.edu/22171/1/1831894%5B1%5D.pdf|archive-date=2015-03-06|citeseerx=10.1.1.475.3740|s2cid=8193254}}</ref> Research conducted by Fischhoff revealed that participants did not know that their outcome knowledge affected their responses, and, if they did know, they could still not ignore or defeat the effects of the bias. Study participants could not accurately reconstruct their previous, less knowledgeable states of mind, which directly relates to the curse of knowledge. This poor reconstruction was theorized by Fischhoff to be because the participant was "anchored in the hindsightful state of mind created by receipt of knowledge".<ref name="Fischhoff">{{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance|date=1975|volume=1|issue=3|pages=288–299|doi=10.1037/0096-1523.1.3.288 }} Reprinted: {{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|pmid=12897366|pmc=1743746|volume=12|issue=4|year=2003|journal=Qual Saf Health Care|pages=304–11|doi=10.1136/qhc.12.4.304}}</ref> This receipt of knowledge returns to the idea of the curse proposed by Camerer, Loewenstein, and Weber: a knowledgeable person cannot accurately reconstruct what a person, be it themselves or someone else, without the knowledge would think, or how they would act. In his paper, Fischhoff questions the failure to empathize with ourselves in less knowledgeable states, and notes that how well people manage to reconstruct perceptions of lesser informed others is a crucial question for historians and "all human understanding".<ref name="Fischhoff" />

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|journal= Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance|date=1975|volume=1|issue=3|pages=288–299|doi=10.1037/0096-1523.1.3.288 }} Reprinted: {{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|pmid=12897366|pmc=1743746|volume=12|issue=4|year=2003|journal=Qual Saf Health Care|pages=304–11|doi=10.1136/qhc.12.4.304}}</ref>
paragraph: Another aspect of the "remember" versus "know" phenomenon is [[hindsight bias]], also referred to as the "knew it all along effect".  This occurs when a person believes that an event is more deterministic after it has happened.<ref name="Radvansky"/> That is, in the face of the outcome of a situation, people tend to overestimate the quality of their previous knowledge, thus leading the person to a distortion towards the provided information. Some researchers believe that the original information gets distorted by the new information at the time of encoding.<ref name="Fischhoff1977">{{cite journal|author=Fischhoff, B. |year=1977|title= Perceived informativeness of facts|doi=10.1037/0096-1523.3.2.349|journal= Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance|volume= 3|issue=2|pages= 349–358}}</ref> The term "creeping determinism" is used to emphasize the fact that it is completely natural for one to integrate outcome information with the original information to create an appropriate whole out of all the pertinent information.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|journal= Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance|date=1975|volume=1|issue=3|pages=288–299|doi=10.1037/0096-1523.1.3.288 }} Reprinted: {{cite journal|last1=Fischhoff|first1=Baruch|title=Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty|pmid=12897366|pmc=1743746|volume=12|issue=4|year=2003|journal=Qual Saf Health Care|pages=304–11|doi=10.1136/qhc.12.4.304}}</ref> Although it was found that informing individuals about the hindsight bias before they took part in experiments did not decrease the bias, it is possible to avoid the effects of the hindsight bias.<ref name="Fischhoff1977"/> Further, by discrediting the outcome knowledge, people are better able to accurately retrieve their original knowledge state, therefore reducing the hindsight bias.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Hasher, L.|year=1981|title= I knew-it-all-along: Or, did I?|url=http://www.psych.utoronto.ca/users/hasher/PDF/I%20knew%20it%20all%20along%20or%20did%20I%20Hasher%20et%20al%201981.pdf|journal=Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior|volume= 20|pages= 86–96|doi=10.1016/s0022-5371(81)90323-6}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Saccade 1989">{{cite journal|last=Rafal |first=Robert |last2=Calabresi |first2=Peter |last3=Brennan |first3=Cameron |last4=Sciolto |first4=Toni |title=Saccade preparation inhibits reorienting to recently attended locations |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology |year=1989 |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=673–685 |pmid=2531204 |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.15.4.673|citeseerx=10.1.1.294.1436 }}</ref>
paragraph:
{{Short description|Cue-response paradigm}}
'''Inhibition of return''' ('''IOR''') refers to an orientation mechanism that briefly enhances (for approximately 100–300 milliseconds (ms)) the speed and accuracy with which an object is detected after the object is attended, but then impairs detection speed and accuracy (for approximately 500–3000 milliseconds). IOR is usually measured with a cue-response paradigm, in which a person presses a button when they detect a target [[Stimulus (psychology)|stimulus]] following the presentation of a cue that indicates the location in which the target will appear. The cue can be [[exogenous]] (or peripheral),<ref name="Posner 1984">{{cite book|last=Posner |first=M.I. |last2=Cohen |first2=Y. |year=1984 |chapter=Components of visual orienting |title=Attention and performance X: Control of language processes |editor-last=Bouma |editor-first=H. |editor-link1=Herman Bouma |editor2-last=Bouwhuis |editor2-first=D. |pages=531–56 |place=Hillsdale, NJ |publisher=Erlbaum}}</ref> or [[endogenous]]. Inhibition of return results from oculomotor activation, regardless of whether it was produced by exogenous signals or endogenously.<ref name="Saccade 1989">{{cite journal|last=Rafal |first=Robert |last2=Calabresi |first2=Peter |last3=Brennan |first3=Cameron |last4=Sciolto |first4=Toni |title=Saccade preparation inhibits reorienting to recently attended locations |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology |year=1989 |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=673–685 |pmid=2531204 |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.15.4.673|citeseerx=10.1.1.294.1436 }}</ref> Although IOR occurs for both visual and auditory stimuli, IOR is greater for visual stimuli,<ref>{{cite journal|last=Reuter-Lorenz |first=P. |last2=Jha |first2=A. |last3=Rosenquist |first3=J.N. |title=What is inhibited in inhibition of return |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology |year=1996 |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=367–378 |pmid=8934850 |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.22.2.367}}</ref> and is studied more often than auditory stimuli.

ref_block: <ref>{{cite journal|last=Chica |first=A. |last2=Klein |first2=R. |last3=Rafal |first3=R. |last4=Hopfinger |first4=J. |title=Endogenous saccade preparation does not produce inhibition of return: Failure to replicate Rafal, Calabresi, Brennan & Sciolto (1989) |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance |year=2010 |volume=36 |issue=5 |pages=1193–1206 |doi=10.1037/a0019951|pmid=20731522 }}</ref>
paragraph:
An early report<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Posner|first1=M. I|last2=Rafal|first2=R. D|last3=Choate|first3=L|last4=Vaughn|first4=J.|title=Inhibition of return: Neural basis and function|journal=Cognitive Neuropsychology|date=1995|volume=2|issue=3|pages=211–228|doi=10.1080/02643298508252866}}</ref> suggested that IOR involves the midbrain [[superior colliculus]]. Support for this suggestion comes from work with a patient who suffered injury to one of the superior colliculi<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Sapir|first1=A|last2=Soroker|first2=N|last3=Berger|first3=A|last4=Henik|first4=A|title=Inhibition of return in spatial attention: Direct evidence for collicular generation|journal=Nature Neuroscience|date=1999|volume=2|issue=12|pages=1053–1054|pmid=10570480|doi=10.1038/15977}}</ref> and experiments with the archer fish.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Gabay|first1=S|last2=Leibovich|first2=T|last3=Ben-Simon|first3=A|last4=Henik|first4=A|last5=Segev|first5=R|title=Inhibition of return in the archer fish|journal=Nature Communications|date=2013|volume=4|doi=10.1038/ncomms2644|pages=1657|pmid=23552072|doi-access=free}}</ref> Moreover, IOR is commonly triggered by an exogenous sensory signal presented in the visual periphery when the eyes are fixed. However, it has been suggested that activation of the midbrain oculomotor pathways might trigger IOR even under endogenous cueing. This conclusion has been questioned by researchers who have found in their studies that endogenous [[saccade]] activation is not efficient to produce IOR.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Chica |first=A. |last2=Klein |first2=R. |last3=Rafal |first3=R. |last4=Hopfinger |first4=J. |title=Endogenous saccade preparation does not produce inhibition of return: Failure to replicate Rafal, Calabresi, Brennan & Sciolto (1989) |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance |year=2010 |volume=36 |issue=5 |pages=1193–1206 |doi=10.1037/a0019951|pmid=20731522 }}</ref>
Others believe that IOR is caused by both a delay in activation of attentional and motor processes.<ref>{{cite book|last=Johnson |first=Addie |last2=Proctor |first2=Robert W. |year=2004 |chapter=Chapter 5: Attention and inhibition |title=Attention: Theory and Practice |pages=127–162 |publisher=Sage Publications |isbn=978-0-7619-2761-7 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Wwu4W_aO8oAC&pg=PA127}}</ref>

ref_block: <ref name="Schmeichel 2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schmeichel BJ | title = Attention control, memory updating, and emotion regulation temporarily reduce the capacity for executive control | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology. General | volume = 136 | issue = 2 | pages = 241–255 | date = May 2007 | pmid = 17500649 | doi = 10.1037/0096-3445.136.2.241 }}</ref>
paragraph: Executive dysfunction does occur to a minor degree in all individuals on both short-term and long-term scales. In non-clinical populations, the activation of executive processes appears to inhibit further activation of the same processes, suggesting a mechanism for normal fluctuations in executive control.<ref name="Schmeichel 2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schmeichel BJ | title = Attention control, memory updating, and emotion regulation temporarily reduce the capacity for executive control | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology. General | volume = 136 | issue = 2 | pages = 241–255 | date = May 2007 | pmid = 17500649 | doi = 10.1037/0096-3445.136.2.241 }}</ref> Decline in executive functioning is also associated with both normal and clinical aging.<ref name="Zelazo et al">{{cite journal | vauthors = Zelazo PD, Craik FI, Booth L | title = Executive function across the life span | journal = Acta Psychologica | volume = 115 | issue = 2–3 | pages = 167–183 | date = February 2004 | pmid = 14962399 | doi = 10.1016/j.actpsy.2003.12.005 | url = https://repositorio.unal.edu.co/handle/unal/81464 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The decline of memory processes as people age appears to affect executive functions, which also points to the general role of memory in executive functioning.<ref name="Bisiacchi">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bisiacchi PS, Borella E, Bergamaschi S, Carretti B, Mondini S | title = Interplay between memory and executive functions in normal and pathological aging | journal = Journal of Clinical and Experimental Neuropsychology | volume = 30 | issue = 6 | pages = 723–733 | date = August 2008 | pmid = 18608665 | doi = 10.1080/13803390701689587 | s2cid = 20708594 }}</ref>
